Finally, we complement the benchmark study with an evaluation on the full UCR archive using localization criteria aligned with sliding-window reconstruction. Overall, our results highlight that reconstruction-based anomaly detection performance depends not only on model architecture and training, but also on inference choices, motivating a clear and reproducible protocol. Our results show that reconstructionbased baselines achieve strong performance on both TSB-AD and UCR benchmarks, supporting them as competitive and practical approaches for univariate time series anomaly detection.

</details>


### [259] [SPACE: Source-free Proxy Anchor Concept Erasure for MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09868)
*Zhijing Zhang,Jiaqi Ding,Qianshan Wei,Nan Zhou,Jiaqi Li,Yongliang Wu,Tongxin Zhu,Xiaolin Fang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces SPACE, a framework for source-free machine unlearning in multimodal large language models (MLLMs), addressing privacy issues without needing access to the target data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address privacy risks and regulatory constraints in MLLMs while ensuring effective unlearning of sensitive data, especially when direct access to the sensitive data is unavailable.

Method: SPACE includes two stages: (1) Text-Guided Proxy Anchor Selection (TPAS), for retrieving semantically aligned proxies, and (2) Dual-Constraint Semantic Isolation (DCSI), for optimizing proxies to indirectly erase target concepts without affecting retained knowledge.

Result: SPACE theoretically ensures minimal impact on retained knowledge and high feature spectral entropy while delivering strong unlearning performance. Experiments on six datasets demonstrate its effectiveness, matching data-dependent methods.

Conclusion: SPACE offers a robust, source-free solution for unlearning sensitive information in MLLMs, balancing privacy protection with maintained model performance.

Abstract: As Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) face growing privacy risks and regulatory constraints, machine unlearning (MU) has emerged as a crucial solution for removing sensitive data while preserving model performance. However, existing MU methods typically rely on visual data of the target concepts, which is often unavailable due to strict data retention policies, thus creating a demand for source-free unlearning approaches that operate without access to the target data. In this work, we propose Source-free Proxy Anchor Concept Erasure (SPACE), the first source-free unlearning framework specialized for MLLMs. SPACE consists of two stages: (1) Text-Guided Proxy Anchor Selection (TPAS), which retrieves semantically aligned proxy anchors from the shared feature space. (2) Dual-Constraint Semantic Isolation (DCSI), which optimizes these anchors to indirectly erase target concepts. DCSI confines updates to the null space of retained knowledge, ensuring structural integrity. We theoretically prove that SPACE strictly bounds the perturbation on retained knowledge and maximizes feature spectral entropy, thereby maintaining the model's performance. Furthermore, extensive experiments across six datasets show that SPACE achieves performance comparable to that of state-of-the-art data-dependent methods, validating its effectiveness in source-free MU scenarios. The source code will be released.

</details>


### [260] [Integrating Local and Global Entropy for Uncertainty Quantification in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09875)
*Johanne Medina,Tianyi Zhou,Keivin Isufaj,Aristides Gionis,Sanjay Chawla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel method combining global and local uncertainty measures to improve uncertainty quantification in large language models, addressing reliability issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models exhibit confident hallucinations, making effective uncertainty quantification critical for ensuring reliable deployment.

Method: The authors propose measuring geometric complexity in hidden-state matrices (global uncertainty) and combining it with token-level uncertainty (local uncertainty) via a new scoring system called Global-Local Uncertainty (GLU).

Result: GLU fuses global and local signals, recovering failure regimes missed by local methods, and achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks while being unsupervised, single-pass, and architecture-agnostic.

Conclusion: Global geometric entropy and token-level entropy are complementary, and combining them (via GLU) enhances failure mode detection and reliability prediction in large language models.

Abstract: Large language models hallucinate confidently, making uncertainty quantification (UQ) essential for reliable deployment. Existing methods rely predominantly on token-level signals, leaving the geometric structure of intermediate hidden states underused. In this paper, we take the geometric complexity of hidden-state matrices as a measure of the global uncertainty of LLMs, while treating token-level uncertainty estimation as a local metric. We show that hidden-state geometric entropy (global uncertainty) and token-level entropy (local uncertainty) are statistically near-orthogonal, capturing distinct failure regimes for reliability prediction. In particular, global geometry recovers the confident-but-wrong failure mode that local signals systematically miss. Building on this, we propose Global-Local Uncertainty (GLU), an unsupervised, single-pass score that fuses the two signals via a multiplicative gate. Across three model families and six benchmarks, GLU matches or outperforms all unsupervised baselines while requiring only a single forward pass and remaining length-normalized and architecture-agnostic.

</details>


### [261] [QSplitFL: Capability Aware Deep Q-Learning for Optimal Split Point Selection in Split Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09869)
*Nazmus Shakib Shadin,Xinyue Zhang,Jingyi Wang,Miao Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QSplitFL is a framework that uses Deep Q-Networks to optimize split points in Split Federated Learning environments based on client hardware metrics, improving efficiency and accuracy in scenarios with heterogeneous devices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation of this paper is to address the challenge of determining optimal split points in Split Federated Learning environments, especially when clients have heterogeneous hardware capabilities. Fixed split points often overload weaker devices and create inefficiencies, necessitating a smarter, capability-aware approach.

Method: The paper proposes QSplitFL, a Deep Q-Network-based framework that derives lightweight state representations from client hardware metrics (e.g., CPU, memory, battery, network latency) to dynamically determine optimal split points. It includes a decayed loss-drop reward function for prioritizing early convergence and a committee-based voting mechanism in its architecture to reduce reward hacking.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple datasets and deep learning architectures indicate that QSplitFL achieves better convergence and higher accuracy compared to existing methods while efficiently adapting to varying device hardware resources.

Conclusion: QSplitFL effectively addresses the challenge of split point selection in Split Federated Learning by leveraging hardware-aware, lightweight state representations. This approach enhances both training efficiency and model performance in heterogeneous environments. The framework offers a practical, publicly available solution for scalable and adaptable distributed learning.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) combined with Split Learning (SL) is a privacy preserving paradigm that enables training deep neural networks (DNNs) on resource constrained devices while reducing overall training cost. However, determining the optimal split point, meaning the layer where the model is divided still remains a critical challenge, especially when clients have heterogeneous hardware capabilities. Fixed split points can overload weak devices and increase the communication and server load, which slows convergence and reduces stability. This paper introduces QSplitFL, a novel capability-aware Deep Q-Network (DQN) framework for optimal split point selection in Split learning based Federated Learning (SFL) environments. Unlike existing approaches that rely on high-dimensional model weight representations, QSplitFL employs a lightweight state representation derived directly from client hardware metrics, including CPU utilization, memory, battery level, and network latency. The proposed framework incorporates a decayed loss-drop reward function that prioritizes early convergence, and a committee-based DQN architecture with majority voting to mitigate reward hacking. Extensive experiments on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 datasets using CNN, ResNet50, MobileNetV4, and ConvNeXt architectures demonstrate that our approach achieves better convergence and higher accuracy compared to existing methods, while effectively adapting to heterogeneous device resources. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/AIPO-Lab/QSplitFL.

</details>


### [262] [TENP: Trapezoidal Expert Neuron Pruning For Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09885)
*Jiangyang He,Shaolin Zhu,Deyi Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes TENP, a novel framework for compressing Mixture-of-Experts LLMs by pruning less important experts and neurons while preserving performance and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind this work is to address the inefficiency caused by the large static parameter footprint of experts in sparse-activated Mixture-of-Experts LLMs.

Method: The authors introduce TENP, a framework that retains important experts and applies structured expert neuron pruning to less essential ones. It considers expert importance based on both output magnitude and the change in input direction, and evaluates neuron importance by projected contribution.

Result: Using TENP, the authors achieve efficient compression: DeepSeek suffers minimal accuracy drop (1-point) with sparse routing and outperforms the full model on certain tasks (10% improvement on code generation).

Conclusion: TENP effectively reduces the parameter footprint of Mixture-of-Experts LLMs without sacrificing accuracy, offering a structured and practical approach to model compression.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts large language models (LLMs) scale efficiently through sparse activation, yet their deployment is fundamentally constrained by the large static parameter footprint of experts. Existing compression approaches either remove entire experts, disrupting routing topology and harming performance, or rely on unstructured weight pruning with limited practical efficiency. To address the limitations, we propose TENP, a structured Trapezoidal ExpertNeuron Pruning framework. Using a few samples, we identify and retain important experts, while applying expert neuron pruning (ENP) to less important experts, reserving model parameters in a trapezoidal pattern from shallow to deep layers. When evaluating expert importance, we jointly consider both the magnitude of the expert output and its ability to change the direction of the input vector. For ENP, we measure each neuron's projected contribution to the expert output to identify and retain important neurons. We conduct extensive experiments on the Qwen and DeepSeek models. Under a routing expert sparsity of 40% and an average of 63.76% activated expert parameters, the DeepSeek model suffers only a 1-point drop in accuracy compared to the full-parameter model. Moreover, it outperforms the full-parameter model by 10% on code generation tasks.

</details>


### [263] [PatchSTG: Scalable Spatiotemporal Graph Transformers for Traffic Forecasting on Irregular Sensor Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09872)
*Jichao Li,Xuanming Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PatchSTG is introduced for efficient traffic forecasting on irregular sensor networks using a hierarchical patch-based spatiotemporal representation and dual attention mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of irregular sensor distribution and high computational costs in traffic forecasting across large-scale and unevenly distributed sensor networks.

Method: PatchSTG uses a hierarchical spatial structure to divide sensors into patches, combined with dual attention mechanisms for local and global dependencies. This results in near-linear computational scaling.

Result: The model performs competitively across multiple forecasting horizons in real-world and large-scale datasets while being computationally more efficient. Ablation studies support the design choices.

Conclusion: The study concludes that patch-based spatiotemporal modeling is a scalable and effective approach for handling traffic forecasting challenges in irregular sensor settings.

Abstract: Traffic forecasting is a fundamental component of intelligent transportation systems, yet remains challenging in real-world settings due to irregular sensor distributions and the high computational cost of modeling large-scale spatiotemporal dependencies. In practical traffic networks, sensors are unevenly distributed across regions, leading to non-uniform spatial structures that limit the effectiveness and scalability of existing graph-based and attention-based models. To address these challenges, we propose PatchSTG, a patch-based spatiotemporal graph Transformer designed for efficient forecasting on irregular sensor networks. The key idea is to introduce a hierarchical spatial representation that partitions sensors into balanced, locality-preserving patches based on geographic information. On top of this structure, a dual attention encoder alternates between intra-patch attention for capturing local interactions and inter-patch attention for modeling global dependencies, reducing computational complexity from quadratic to near-linear scaling. We evaluate PatchSTG on real-world traffic data from Rhode Island and additional large-scale datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves stable and competitive forecasting performance across multiple horizons, while significantly improving computational efficiency. Ablation studies further validate the effectiveness of spatial partitioning and dual attention in capturing both local and long-range traffic dynamics. These results suggest that patch-based spatiotemporal modeling provides a scalable and effective framework for traffic forecasting under irregular spatial settings.

</details>


### [264] [Inverse Probability Weighting and Age-of-Information Aggregation for Decentralized Federated Learning under Partial Reception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10774)
*Chanuka A. S. Hewa Kaluannakkage,Rajkumar Buyya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes DFL-AA, a method to address selection bias and update staleness in Decentralized Federated Learning over wireless networks, offering solutions to improve model aggregation and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation lies in addressing the challenges of selection bias and update staleness in decentralized federated learning, which result from lossy wireless links and asynchronous updates.

Method: The proposed method, DFL-AA, corrects selection bias using Inverse Probability Weighting and mitigates staleness through Age-of-Information-based weighting, enhanced by online EWMA-based channel estimation.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows that DFL-AA removes link-quality distortion in expectation, and experiments demonstrate performance improvements over existing methods under diverse conditions.

Conclusion: DFL-AA effectively tackles persistent issues in decentralized federated learning, offering a robust solution for aggregating updates in lossy and heterogeneous wireless networks.

Abstract: Decentralized Federated Learning (DFL) over lossy wireless networks faces two key challenges: selection bias, where updates from poor-quality links are systematically underrepresented due to partial model reception, and update staleness, where asynchronous nodes contribute outdated information. We show that uniform gossip aggregation with local-fill reconstruction introduces persistent link-quality-induced bias, while completeness-based weighting further amplifies this effect. To address these challenges, we propose DFL-AA (Decentralized Federated Learning with Adaptive AoI-weighted Aggregation), which combines Inverse Probability Weighting with online EWMA-based channel estimation to correct selection bias and Age-of-Information-based weighting to mitigate staleness without requiring global synchronization. We theoretically show that DFL-AA removes link-quality distortion in expectation and experimentally demonstrate consistent improvements over state-of-the-art baselines across varying loss rates, network sizes, and heterogeneous wireless conditions.

</details>


### [265] [Nonlinear Estimator: Dual Bayesian Affine Estimators for Parameter Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10111)
*Sasan Vakili,Daniël Woonings,Pradyumna Paruchuri,Peyman Mohajerin Esfahani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces two nonlinear parameter estimation methods for Wiener-type state-space models, showing significant improvements in performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve the parameter estimation for Wiener-type state-space models by leveraging nonlinear dynamic behaviors in a computationally efficient manner.

Method: The authors develop two nonlinear estimation architectures—dual basis-parameter and dual state-parameter estimators—using dynamic basis statistics (DBS) and fixed-point iterations.

Result: Monte Carlo experiments demonstrate that the dual state-parameter estimator achieves the best performance in terms of parameter mean-squared error, outperforming existing methods.

Conclusion: The proposed dual estimators effectively enhance parameter estimation accuracy, particularly the dual state-parameter estimator, which excels beyond competing affine and sequential methods.

Abstract: This paper presents a nonlinear parameter estimator for Wiener-type state-space models obtained as a fixed-point architecture that couples two affine minimum mean-squared error (MMSE) estimators: one for the unknown parameters and one for latent variables. The architecture retains the functional structure of the optimal affine MMSE parameter estimator while incorporating Dynamic Basis Statistics (DBS) estimates that summarize nonlinear basis-function evaluations. Two DBS construction strategies are developed, leading to two nonlinear estimator frameworks. The dual basis-parameter estimator combines an affine basis estimator with the affine parameter estimator, whereas the dual state-parameter estimator first computes affine state estimates and their covariances, then maps these state-estimate statistics through a Gaussian DBS operator to obtain DBS estimates. Both dual estimators admit fixed-point characterizations that alternate between estimating each component using the updated prior of the other, obtained from that component's plug-in estimate statistics from the previous iteration. The efficacy of the proposed methods is examined via extensive Monte Carlo experiments, showing that the dual basis-parameter estimator attains parameter mean-squared errors comparable to those of the purely affine parameter estimator, while the dual state-parameter estimator achieves the lowest parameter mean-squared error, outperforming both the dual basis-parameter and purely affine parameter estimators, as well as sequential Monte Carlo variants of classical Particle Gibbs and Expectation-Maximization schemes.

</details>


### [266] [Rotate2Think: Geometric Priming via Orthogonal Rotation to Improve Language Model Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09873)
*Aditya Sharma,Christopher J. Pal,Amal Zouaq*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a method to enhance reasoning model performance by studying the geometric structure of embeddings during reasoning and applying a rotation-based adjustment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to better understand the internal representation space of reasoning models and leverage this knowledge to improve reasoning accuracy during inference.

Method: The authors observed that reasoning embeddings form a distinct geometric structure separate from input embeddings. They developed Rotate2Think, a method using orthogonal Procrustes analysis to estimate a rotation for transitioning from input to reasoning space, which primes reasoning at inference without additional training.

Result: Rotate2Think improved reasoning accuracy across 30 of 32 evaluated model-benchmark configurations and demonstrated zero-shot generalization to multimodal tasks like MATH-Vision.

Conclusion: Exploiting the geometric distinction between input and reasoning embeddings with Rotate2Think enhances reasoning performance on diverse tasks, showcasing the utility of understanding hidden representation structures.

Abstract: Reasoning models achieve strong performance on challenging tasks by generating explicit intermediate reasoning traces before producing a final answer. Yet the internal structure of representation space when reasoning remains poorly understood: how do a model's hidden representations differ during thinking versus the embeddings of the input prompt, and can this structure be exploited to elicit stronger reasoning at inference time? We show that both input embeddings and thinking embeddings (mean-pooled last-layer hidden states over the prompt and reasoning trace, respectively) exhibit extremely high conicity, with all vectors clustering tightly around a single mean direction. Crucially, these mean input and thinking directions are non-collinear, with thinking embeddings occupying a geometrically distinct region of embedding space across many different models and benchmark tasks. This observation motivates casting the input-to-thinking transition as a rotation problem admitting a closed-form solution via orthogonal Procrustes analysis. We propose Rotate2Think, a training-free method that estimates this rotation from a small set of correctly solved examples and injects the resulting synthetic thinking vector between thinking delimiters at inference time, providing a geometric primer at the onset of the reasoning trace. Evaluated across multiple benchmarks and model families, Rotate2Think improves accuracy in 30 of 32 model-benchmark configurations across mathematics, science, and code tasks, and generalizes zero-shot to multimodal reasoning on MATH-Vision.

</details>


### [267] [Rank Collapse, Fixed Points, and the Renormalization Group Structure of MLP Residual Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10324)
*Parviz Haggi-Mani,Irina Rish*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper explores the analogy between deep neural networks (DNNs) and the renormalization group (RG) by focusing on pure MLP residual stacks and empirically verifies the selective coarse-graining mechanism influenced by input spectral structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to quantitatively investigate the analogy between neural network layer progression and RG flows, which has been qualitatively noted but lacked empirical validation.

Method: Researchers trained pure MLP residual stacks on masked token prediction tasks using synthetic Markov chain sequences and analyzed the effective rank, kernel drift, and preservation of relevant degrees under controlled input variations.

Result: The findings include a monotonic rank collapse in residual streams, selective coarse-graining aligned with RG criteria depending on input correlation length, and inter-layer kernel drift localized at transitions while maintaining near-fixed states elsewhere in the network.

Conclusion: The study provides empirical evidence that deep MLP residual networks implement a coarse-graining procedure governed by structural properties of the input, advancing both theoretical understanding and practical design implications in neural networks.

Abstract: The analogy between deep neural network forward passes and renormalization group (RG) flows has been repeatedly noted in the literature, but existing treatments remain qualitative: depth is described as a coarse-graining scale, attention is likened to a partition function, and representations are said to flow toward fixed points. No existing work has defined a measurable RG order parameter, tested it under controlled variation of the input distribution, or made quantitative predictions that are empirically verified. We study the simplest architecture for which the analogy is tractable: a pure MLP residual stack trained on masked token prediction over synthetic Markov chain sequences with known spectral properties. We report three findings. (i) The effective rank of the residual stream decreases monotonically with depth after training, consistent with progressive integration of irrelevant degrees of freedom. (ii) This rank collapse is selective: it occurs for chains with short correlation length approximately 1 but is absent for chains with long correlation length approximately 7, measured at the position level to control for mean-pooling artifacts. The network preserves exactly the degrees of freedom relevant to the prediction task, the content of the RG relevance criterion. (iii) Inter-layer kernel drift is concentrated at one or two specific transitions, with the remainder of the network near a fixed point, consistent with a discrete fixed-point plateau. Together these findings constitute the first quantitative, position-level evidence that MLP residual networks implement a selective coarse-graining procedure governed by the spectral structure of the input distribution.

</details>


### [268] [Calibrating Overconfidence Without Sacrificing Confidence: Probe-Conditioned Head Intervention for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09876)
*Ke Li,Chongzhe Zhang,Zifan Zeng,Feng Liu,Qunli Zhang,Zheng Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method to identify and reduce overconfidence in large language models, specifically targeting wrong but confident responses, without significantly affecting warranted confidence in correct answers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the problem of large language models expressing high confidence in wrong answers, which is exacerbated by standard global calibration methods that harm both warranted and unwarranted confidence levels equally.

Method: The method, Probe-Conditioned Head Intervention (PCHI), uses a frozen probe to detect likely wrong-but-confident responses and conditionally intervenes in attention-head outputs during confidence detection.

Result: PCHI effectively converts 82.2% of wrong-but-confident responses from 'yes' to 'no' on Qwen3-4B-Instruct, reduces calibration error from 21.9% to 9.2%, and minimally harms correct-but-confident responses (5.1% damage). It shows similar but weaker effects on another model, Gemma3-4B.

Conclusion: Selective internal intervention via PCHI can reduce verbalized overconfidence in models, allowing for a more nuanced decoupling of reducing unwarranted confidence without losing warranted confidence.

Abstract: Large language models often express high confidence in answers that are wrong. Standard calibration remedies typically act globally or at the score level, reducing unwarranted confidence but also risking erosion of warranted confidence on correct answers. We introduce Probe-Conditioned Head Intervention (PCHI), an inference-time method that uses a frozen probe to detect likely wrong-but-confident responses and conditionally rescales downstream attention-head outputs during confidence generation. On Qwen3-4B-Instruct solving OpenMathInstruct problems with a structured binary confidence field, readout-token PCHI converts 82.2% of originally wrong-yes confidence readouts to $\texttt{no}$, while a joint intervention across upstream confidence-template tokens reduces ECE from 21.9% to 9.2% and damages only 5.1% of originally correct-yes readouts. The readout-token effect also appears on Gemma3-4B, though upstream interventions are weaker and more mask-dependent. These results show that verbalized overconfidence can be selectively reduced through conditionally applied internal intervention, partially decoupling the suppression of unwarranted confidence from the loss of warranted confidence.

</details>


### [269] [Advancing the State-of-the-Art in Empirical Privacy Auditing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10481)
*Nicole Mitchell,Galen Andrew,Arun Ganesh,Brendan McMahan,Peter Kairouz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses privacy risks in fine-tuning large language models (LLMs), proposing synthetic canaries for robust privacy audits and investigating memorization dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mitigate and measure privacy risks posed by fine-tuned LLMs, particularly concerning memorization of sensitive training examples.

Method: High-temperature sampling from LLMs generates synthetic canaries for privacy auditing, and an auxiliary auditing method is developed for synthetic data.

Result: The introduced methods ensure high identifiability of private data and enable systematic exploration of factors affecting memorization in LLMs.

Conclusion: Synthetic canaries and strong privacy-auditing methodologies provide effective tools for evaluating and addressing privacy risks in LLM fine-tuning and synthetic data generation.

Abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs) can exhibit problematic memorization of individual training examples. Empirical privacy auditing (EPA) quantifies this risk by measuring realistic data leakage on membership inference (MI) or reconstruction attacks. A key challenge in EPA is designing ``canary'' examples that are mixed with the privacy-sensitive training data. We propose generating synthetic canaries via high-temperature sampling ($T \geq 0.8$) from LLMs, using prompts tailored to the privacy-sensitive training data. These canaries act as high-influence outliers, ensuring high identifiability and hence strong audits. Further, since the canaries are themselves non-private, they are inspectable and can be inserted with repetition without jeopardizing the privacy of the real data. An important use of models fine-tuned on privacy-sensitive data is the generation of synthetic data. This also comes with privacy risk. We introduce a powerful synthetic data audit based on fine-tuning an auxiliary model on the synthetic data. Auditing the auxiliary model for the original canaries then provides a strong estimate of the privacy leakage through the synthetic data. Finally, leveraging our strong auditing methodologies, we perform a systematic investigation into the interacting effects of model capacity and canary entropy on memorization.

</details>


### [270] [Streaming Knowledge Compilation: Proactive Materiality-Scored Pinning for Time-Evolving LLM Wikis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09877)
*Juan M. Huerta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a method for maintaining an up-to-date LLM knowledge base from a document stream in dynamic environments, tackling cumulative regret in knowledge retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM wiki systems work on a static corpus, which becomes inadequate as information evolves over time. The paper introduces a dynamic approach to adaptively compile knowledge while minimizing inefficiency in inference.

Method: The authors propose the Streaming Knowledge Compilation framework using a materiality signal to score document importance for entities. The method offers theoretical guarantees and domain-specific implementations in finance and Wikipedia to prove its universality.

Result: Empirical validation shows effective prediction of knowledge gaps via domain-specific signals. It includes achieving significant volatility predictions in finance and effective edit tracking in Wikipedia while converging cumulative regret demonstrates system adaptation.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that adaptive knowledge systems can mitigate inefficiencies with minimal prior domain knowledge. Regret analysis emerges as a critical evaluation metric in such systems, outperforming traditional QA score comparisons.

Abstract: LLM wiki systems compile knowledge into pre-filled KV caches for efficient inference, but assume a static corpus -- an assumption that fails whenever the underlying information landscape evolves. We formalize Streaming Knowledge Compilation: given a document stream, a fixed token budget, and future queries unknown at ingestion time, maintain a compiled wiki that minimizes cumulative regret against an offline oracle with perfect foresight. The enabling insight is a materiality signal $φ_t(k,n)\in[0,1]$ that scores document importance for entity $k$ at time $t$, acting as a query-relevance surrogate for proactive pinning before queries arrive; we prove an $O(\sqrt{T\log K})$ regret bound where $\varepsilon=\mathbb{E}[|φ_t-\hatφ_t|]$ is the only domain-specific quantity. We instantiate in two domains: finance, where $φ_t$ is abnormal stock volatility predicted by frozen Llama 3.1 8B classification head (AUROC = 0.728 on 76K articles, strict temporal split; $1.49\times$ higher realized forward volatility for predicted-material articles); and Wikipedia, where $φ_t$ is the Abnormal Edit Ratio (AER), a cross-sectionally normalized edit velocity -- showing the same algorithm generalizes beyond the finance domain. End-to-end QA evaluation on 173 matched pairs (finance) and 119 (Wikipedia) reveals a pervasive LLM-as-judge confound on post-training knowledge, establishing that regret analysis -- not absolute QA scores -- is the reliable evaluation metric for compiled knowledge systems. Finance cumulative regret converges to -20.0 (-0.12/step); Wikipedia to +16.0 (+0.13/step), with the positive sign confirming that Wikipedia edit content is genuinely post-training -- richer context consistently improves scores (No Wiki 3.80 vs. Oracle 4.74) -- and eliminates this confound. The $O(\sqrt{T\log K})$ guarantee applies to any domain where knowledge gaps can be predicted from streaming signals.

</details>


### [271] [FailureScope: Cross-Regime Behavioral Diagnosis of Language Model Weaknesses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09878)
*Nicholas Saban*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces FailureScope, a method for identifying specific model failures through behavioral clustering across various evaluation regimes, demonstrating its generalizability and effective clustering capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for a more detailed understanding of model weaknesses beyond aggregate accuracy metrics reported by standard benchmarks.

Method: The authors propose FailureScope, a behavioral-diagnosis method that clusters evaluation tasks by cross-model pass/fail patterns using a leave-one-model-out (LOMO) sampling strategy. They test it across three evaluation regimes: single-turn benchmarks, multi-turn dialogue, and adversarial agent attacks.

Result: FailureScope achieves high interpretability and predictive stability with success across single-turn tasks (Kendall's tau = 0.81, AUC 0.88), multi-turn dialogues, and adversarial agent settings. It also reveals a significant meta-failure mode in agent evaluations.

Conclusion: FailureScope provides a portable and effective tool for diagnosing model failures, capable of producing stable and interpretable taxonomies across various settings. The authors release their pipeline, corpora, and taxonomies to support further research.

Abstract: Standard benchmarks report aggregate accuracy, but practitioners need to know which specific capabilities a model lacks. We introduce FailureScope, a behavioral-diagnosis method that clusters evaluation probes by their cross-model pass/fail patterns (leave-one-model-out, LOMO), and show it yields stable, interpretable failure taxonomies across three regimes usually studied separately: single-turn benchmarks, multi-turn dialogue, and adversarial agent attacks. On 2,664 single-turn tasks across 18 models, taxonomy-conditioned sampling reaches Kendall's tau = 0.81 at 50 tasks (versus 0.34 for random selection), and cross-model failure prediction reaches AUC 0.88. The same primitive recovers interpretable clusters on a 363-task multi-turn corpus and on 630 adversarial agent traces, where it exposes a meta-failure mode: a 73-100 percentage-point gap between LLM-judge ASR and real execution. Cluster cohesion remains strong across all three regimes, which we take as evidence that behavioral clustering is a portable diagnosis primitive that generalizes beyond any single benchmark. We release the pipeline, three annotated corpora, and the cross-regime taxonomies.

</details>


### [272] [SPACR: Single-Pass Adaptive Training of Uncertainty-Aware Conformal Regressors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10734)
*Soundouss Messoudi,Sylvain Rousseau,Sébastien Destercke*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conformal Prediction traditionally applied post hoc is improved by SPACR, which trains directly for uncertainty-aware regressors providing efficient intervals at multiple confidence levels without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional Conformal Prediction methods are misaligned with their goal of efficient uncertainty intervals since they are applied post hoc and require costly retraining for different confidence levels.

Method: SPACR introduces a single-pass approach that integrates training for uncertainty-aware regressors into a differentiable loss function, optimizing efficiency and validity simultaneously while supporting multiple confidence levels without batch-splitting.

Result: SPACR outperforms standard Conformal Prediction and DOICR by producing tighter prediction intervals, better coverage-efficiency trade-offs, and significantly reducing computational costs.

Conclusion: SPACR effectively aligns model training with conformal prediction goals, offering improved prediction interval efficiency and flexibility, and reduces the computational burden of previous methods such as DOICR.

Abstract: Conformal Prediction (CP) provides robust uncertainty guarantees for predictive models, but is typically applied post hoc, which misaligns model training with the conformal goal of producing efficient (i.e, narrow) intervals. We propose SPACR (Single-Pass Adaptive Conformal Regressor), a novel method for directly training uncertainty-aware regressors within a differentiable loss. SPACR jointly optimizes efficiency and validity without batch-splitting or a predefined confidence levels during training. As a result, a single SPACR model yields valid prediction intervals at multiple confidence levels during inference, avoiding the costly retraining required by methods like DOICR. Experiments on diverse datasets show that SPACR consistently gives tighter intervals and better coverage-efficiency trade-offs compared to standard CP and DOICR, while significantly reducing computational costs.

</details>


### [273] [Operator Fusion for LLM Inference on the Tensix Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09879)
*Qingbo Wu,Ke Li,Wenzhu Wang,Jie Yu,Ruian Zhang,Lili Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a method to reduce latency and improve efficiency in Transformer models through operator fusion and optimized multi-core parallelism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome inference bottlenecks in Transformer models on the Tensix architecture, particularly focusing on reducing DRAM operations and optimizing data movement.

Method: The authors fuse RMSNorm with matrix multiplication in both self-attention and FFN, enabling efficient execution of memory/computation-bound operations. They also implement a multicast mechanism for better multi-core parallelism.

Result: The proposed method achieves up to 37.44% latency reduction in self-attention, 15.89% in the MLP, and up to 7.91% reduction per decoder layer, with minimal numerical deviation (PCC > 98.75%).

Conclusion: The approach significantly enhances end-to-end efficiency and reduces latency in Transformer models while maintaining numerical accuracy.

Abstract: This study addresses on-device inference bottlenecks of Transformer models on Tenstorrent's Tensix architecture and proposes an operator fusion strategy that enhances data locality. RMSNorm is fused with matrix multiplication in self-attention and in the FFN, enabling back-to-back execution of memory-bound and compute-bound operators in on-chip SRAM to significantly reduce DRAM reads/writes of intermediate results and scheduling overhead. To support multi-core parallelism, a NoC-based multicast mechanism is leveraged in which row/column master nodes efficiently distribute inputs and weights across the core mesh, alleviating DRAM bandwidth contention. Experiments on the Wormhole platform with Qwen2.5-0.5B, Qwen3-0.6B, and Qwen3-4B show up to 37.44% latency reduction for attention and 15.89% for MLP, with up to 7.91% reduction per decoder layer, while Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC) remains above 98.75%, confirming significant end-to-end efficiency gains under numerical consistency.

</details>


### [274] [Conservation Laws from Data Symmetry in Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10913)
*Jakob Galley,Vahid Shahverdi,Axel Flinth*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates how intrinsic symmetries in training data affect conserved quantities during neural network training. Data symmetries rarely produce additional conserved quantities unless specific loss functions and network structures are used.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the relationship between data symmetries and conserved quantities in neural network training, which could lead to more efficient training methods and deeper theoretical insights.

Method: The authors analyze neural network training dynamics assuming an analytic and non-polynomial loss function. They propose the concept of 'tensorizable networks' to explore these phenomena, including linear, polynomial networks and Lightning Attention.

Result: They find that for typical analytic loss functions, data symmetries generally do not lead to additional conserved quantities. However, with mean squared error (MSE) loss, certain data augmentation strategies can produce extra conserved quantities.

Conclusion: Symmetry properties in data have nuanced implications on conserved quantities, particularly depending on the network architecture and loss function. Tensorizable networks provide a useful framework for studying these dynamics.

Abstract: We explore whether intrinsic symmetries of the training data lead to conserved quantities during gradient-flow training of neural networks. Under the assumption that the loss function is analytic and non-polynomial, we prove that data symmetries generically do not induce any additional integrals of motion. For mean squared error (MSE) loss, on the other hand, there are situations in which data augmentation yields extra conserved quantities. We build a framework, utilizing \emph{tensorizable networks} to describe this phenomenon. Tensorizable networks are a family of architectures whose dependence on parameters and inputs can be separated using an intermediate representation. They include linear and polynomial networks, as well as Lightning Attention.

</details>


### [275] [Hyperparameter Learning for Latent Factorization of Tensors for Representation Learning to Large-scale Dynamic Weighted Directed Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09880)
*Yaqian Zhan,Jialan He,Tianzhu Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces an automated hyperparameter optimization framework based on Differential Evolution for Latent Factorization of Tensors (DE-LFT) to improve the performance on dynamic weighted directed networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The performance of latent factorization of tensors (LFT) heavily depends on the selection of hyperparameters, which is typically done manually or with labor-intensive methods like grid search.

Method: The paper integrates Differential Evolution (DE) into the LFT training process to automatically optimize hyperparameters $λ_1$, $λ_2$, and $λ_3$, enabling adaptive hyperparameter search.

Result: The framework achieves lower Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) on four real-world datasets versus manually tuned models, while reducing the manual effort needed for parameter tuning.

Conclusion: Automating hyperparameter optimization using Differential Evolution significantly improves LFT model performance and reduces reliance on manual tuning.

Abstract: Large-scale dynamic weighted directed networks (DWDNs) are widely used to model time-varying interactions among nodes. Latent factorization of tensors (LFT) extracts target knowledge from DWDNs via low-rank embedding. However, similar to many machine learning models, the performance of LFT heavily depends on the selection of hyperparameters. In practice, these parameters are often tuned manually or through grid search, which requires significant computational resources and human effort. Motivated by this challenge, this paper proposes an automated hyperparameter optimization framework based on Differential Evolution (DE) for LFT (DE-LFT). The proposed method integrates DE into the training process of the LFT model to automatically learn optimal regularization parameters $λ_1$, $λ_2$ and $λ_3$. As a result, the model can adaptively search the hyperparameter space and improve prediction accuracy. Experimental results on four real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves lower MAE and RMSE compared with manually tuned baselines while reducing the need for extensive parameter tuning.

</details>


### [276] [Express Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10944)
*Albert Gong,Annabelle Michael Carrell,Raaz Dwivedi,Lester Mackey*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new tool, Express, converts non-causal attention approximations into causal approximations with state-of-the-art guarantees, improving memory and computational efficiency for sequence processing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address resource bottlenecks in the language modeling pipeline and improve efficiency in handling long-context sequences.

Method: The authors introduce Express, which enhances a Thinformer approximation for causal attention, ensuring improved error bounds and resource efficiency, combined with a Triton implementation for practical speedups.

Result: Express achieves $log^{3/2}(n)/s$ approximation error with $O(s)$ memory and $O(s^2 log^2(n))$ overhead, shows speedups over FlashAttention 2, and resolves multiple language modeling resource challenges.

Conclusion: Express effectively optimizes the handling of causal attention with improved guarantees and broad applicability in long-sequence language modeling tasks.

Abstract: We introduce a new tool, Express, for converting a non-causal attention approximation into a causal approximation with matching approximation guarantees. When combined with the state-of-the-art Thinformer approximation, Express improves upon the best known causal attention guarantees, delivering $\log^{3/2}(n)/s$ approximation error with only $O(s)$ memory and $O(s^2 \log^2(n))$ compression overhead for a sequence of length $n$. We pair these developments with an efficient I/O-aware Triton implementation, demonstrate substantial speedups over FlashAttention 2, and use Express to overcome four resource bottlenecks in the language modeling pipeline: long-context prefill, KV cache compression, long-form memory-constrained decoding, and long-form compute-constrained decoding.

</details>


### [277] [Toward Calibrated, Fair, and accurate Deepfake Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09881)
*Ryan Brown,Chris Russell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Face-Fairness (FF), a fairness-improving framework for deepfake detection that doesn't rely on demographic labels, retraining, or sacrificing overall accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the bias in deepfake detector performance across demographic groups without requiring demographic labels, retraining, or sacrificing accuracy.

Method: The authors propose Face-Feature Tuning (FFT), a lightweight calibrator that adjusts logits based on frozen face embeddings, complemented by two variants for group discovery and enhancement.

Result: Face-Fairness (FF) reduces false positive/true positive rate gaps, improves minimum group accuracy, and often enhances overall accuracy in in-domain and cross-dataset tests.

Conclusion: FF is a plug-and-play, detector-agnostic solution requiring minimal runtime overhead and no access to demographic attributes, making it a practical tool for fairness in deepfake detection.

Abstract: Deepfake detectors show large performance gaps across demographic groups. Existing fairness approaches require demographic labels, retraining, or sacrifice accuracy. We introduce Face-Fairness (FF), a plug-and-play framework for bias mitigation. Our primary contribution, Face-Feature Tuning (FFT), is the first demographic label-free fairness method demonstrated for deepfake detection: a lightweight calibrator that performs a logit remapping conditioned on frozen face embeddings. We complement FFT with two variants: FF-Max, which maximizes worst-group accuracy when demographics are available, and FF-Discover, which does the same with embedding-discovered groups. Across in-domain and cross-dataset test settings, FF consistently reduces FPR/TPR gaps and improves minimum group accuracy while maintaining (often improving) overall accuracy. The approach is detector-agnostic, adds negligible runtime overhead, and requires no access to identity attributes.

</details>


### [278] [Flexible Kernels for Protein Property Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11057)
*Martin Jankowiak,Yerdos Ordabayev,Rudraksh Tuwani,Henry N. Ward,Hunter Nisonoff,James M. McFarland,Gevorg Grigoryan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces sequence kernels combined with Gaussian processes for protein property prediction, outperforming methods that use foundation model embeddings and excelling in multi-task learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of predicting protein properties like binding affinity and thermostability with sparse experimental data by improving model efficiency and accuracy.

Method: Developed sequence kernels exploiting evolutionary substitution matrices and local linearity, integrating structural information from foundation models, and applying these to Gaussian processes.

Result: The proposed method frequently outperforms foundation model embeddings and excels in multi-task learning across multiple protein properties.

Conclusion: Structure-aware, substitution matrix-based sequence kernels offer a data-efficient, accurate way to model protein property landscapes, particularly in multi-task scenarios.

Abstract: Despite its importance to applications in protein design, predicting protein properties like binding affinity and thermostability from sparse experimental data remains a significant challenge. Accordingly, we introduce a class of sequence kernels that exploit evolutionary substitution matrices as well as local linearity and demonstrate that the resulting Gaussian processes provide data-efficient models of protein property landscapes, frequently outperforming alternatives that rely on foundation model embeddings. Furthermore--by learning what are in effect structure-aware substitution matrices--we show that our kernels can readily incorporate structural information from foundation models. We demonstrate that these structure-conditioned kernels are well suited to multi-task learning across multiple protein property landscapes and can decisively outperform local supervised learning methods.

</details>


### [279] [TD-Grokking: Learning from Zero-Reward Problems by Training-Time Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09883)
*Ningyuan Xi,Hao Xu,Hongsheng Xin,Ning Miao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces TD-Grokking, a training-time decomposition framework that breaks down unsolvable zero-reward problems into smaller, solvable subproblems, offering optimization signals to improve reasoning tasks in LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards struggles with zero-reward problems where there is no optimization signal. Existing solutions fail to fully address this challenge, limiting LLMs' ability to handle intractable tasks.

Method: TD-Grokking recursively decomposes root problems into hierarchical trees of verifiable subproblems, enabling reward signals to emerge from solvable leaves. These rewards guide optimization during training.

Result: The framework demonstrated improved performance over vanilla GRPO and other baseline methods in mathematical and medical tasks, as shown through evaluations and detailed analysis.

Conclusion: TD-Grokking effectively converts zero-reward problems into solvable subproblems, offering a robust solution to a critical limitation in RLVR, leading to consistent performance improvements in reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have made remarkable progress in reasoning tasks, largely driven by post-training paradigms, especially reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). However, a critical bottleneck persists: RLVR fails on highly challenging zero-reward problems, where all sampled reasoning trajectories yield uniformly failed outcomes, providing no optimization signal to drive model improvement. Prior efforts to address this limitation, such as dense process supervision, partial reward assignment, or prefix-guided exploration, suffer from inherent task constraints or do not fully equip the policy model with the capabilities necessary to solve the original intractable problems. To address this, we propose TD-Grokking, a training-time decomposition framework for zero-reward problems. It recursively decomposes intractable root problems into self-contained, verifiable subproblems, forming hierarchical trees where solvable leaves provide non-zero rewards. Evaluations on mathematical and medical tasks show that TD-Grokking outperforms vanilla GRPO as well as all baseline approaches. Together with detailed analysis, these results confirm that training-time decomposition effectively converts zero-reward examples into usable training signals, enabling consistent performance gains. Our code and datasets are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TD-Grokking-6567/.

</details>


### [280] [Limitations of Learning Tanh Neural Networks with Finite Precision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11104)
*Philipp Grohs,Matěj Trödler*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper establishes limitations on learning $\tanh$ neural networks with finite precision, proving that adaptive algorithms cannot exceed Monte Carlo convergence rates in $L^p$ norm without an exponential increase in sampling cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the intrinsic limitations of learning $\tanh$ neural networks under practical constraints, including finite precision calculations and bounded sampling budgets.

Method: A novel construction of sharply localized bump functions through iterated $\tanh$ activations is utilized to theoretically analyze adaptive randomized algorithms' performance under finite precision.

Result: The paper demonstrates that adaptive randomized algorithms cannot surpass the Monte Carlo convergence rate $O(m^{-1/p})$ in learning $\tanh$ networks unless the sampling budget scales exponentially with network size.

Conclusion: Finite precision imposes fundamental restrictions on learning localized bump functions in $\tanh$ networks, generalizing previous findings for ReLU networks to this new setting.

Abstract: We investigate limitations of learning $\tanh$ neural networks from point evaluations under finite-precision computations and $L^p$ accuracy guarantees, building on Berner, Grohs, and Voigtländer (2023). Our approach is based on a novel construction of sharply localized bump functions via iterated $\tanh$ activations. Using this mechanism, we show that, in a finite-precision setting, no adaptive randomized algorithm based on $m$ samples can achieve a convergence rate higher than the Monte Carlo rate $O(m^{-1/p})$ in the $L^p$ norm, unless the sampling budget grows exponentially with the size of the network parameters and architecture. The results reveal fundamental limitations imposed by finite precision on the learnability of classes containing localized bump functions, extending previous results for ReLU networks to the $\tanh$ setting.

</details>


### [281] [Data-Driven Dynamic Assortment in Online Platforms: Learning about Two Sides](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11118)
*Rahul Roy,Nur Sunar,Jayashankar M. Swaminathan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study addresses optimal assortment selection on a two-sided service platform with unknown customer and seller preferences, proposing a learning algorithm with polylogarithmic worst-case regret.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To manage dynamic assortment decisions in service platforms where customer and seller preferences are unknown and develop an efficient and adaptable solution.

Method: The authors propose a data-driven algorithm that simultaneously learns the choice models of customers and sellers while optimizing the platform's revenue over time.

Result: The algorithm achieves worst-case regret that grows polylogarithmically with time, supported by a matching theoretical lower bound demonstrating rate optimality.

Conclusion: This study introduces a rate-optimal approach to solving dynamic assortment problems with incomplete choice parameter data, providing a framework to minimize revenue loss efficiently.

Abstract: We study a dynamic assortment problem on a two-sided service platform with incomplete information and heterogeneous customers in a discrete-time setting. In each period, a customer arrives seeking service, and the platform chooses an assortment of sellers to display. The customer then proposes a transaction to at most one seller in the assortment according to a multinomial logit choice model. After a fixed number of periods, sellers review the proposals they have received and each chooses at most one customer according to another multinomial logit choice model, after which the cycle repeats. A key challenge is that the platform does not know the choice-model parameters of either customers or sellers in advance. To our knowledge, this is the first study of a dynamic assortment problem in which both sides' choice parameters are unknown. We develop a data-driven algorithm that learns these parameters while optimizing the platform's objective over time. We evaluate performance using regret, which measures revenue loss relative to a clairvoyant benchmark that knows all parameters and customer arrivals in advance. We show that the algorithm's worst-case regret grows polylogarithmically over time, and we derive a matching lower bound, establishing its rate optimality.

</details>


### [282] [SHAPE: Coalition-Aware Expert Pruning for Sparse Mixture-of-Experts LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09886)
*Yuhao Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SHAPE is a pruning framework for Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) large language models that accounts for expert cooperation, improving memory efficiency and computation without significant accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the memory limitations of deploying Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models by efficiently pruning experts while considering their cooperative contributions.

Method: SHAPE applies a Shapley-value-based attribution to model intra-layer cooperation of experts using routing traces from a small calibration set. It uses a quality-coverage selection rule to prune experts globally, ensuring minimal subsets are retained while maintaining task performance.

Result: SHAPE demonstrates robust performance by maintaining competitive accuracy even with 20% to 40% of experts pruned, reducing GPU memory usage significantly across several large MoE models and benchmarks.

Conclusion: SHAPE improves the practicality of deploying MoE models by providing an effective expert pruning strategy that maintains model quality and reduces resource demands. It is publicly available for further research.

Abstract: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) large language models achieve strong quality with low per-token compute, yet their deployment is often limited by the memory wall: the full expert pool must remain resident to support token-dependent routing. Expert pruning is a direct remedy, but prior criteria typically score experts independently and overlook that MoE inference is inherently \emph{coalitional}, where outputs arise from routed top-$k$ expert combinations. We propose \textbf{SHAPE}, a task-driven pruning framework that explicitly models \emph{intra-layer} expert cooperation. SHAPE formulates routing traces on a small calibration set as an empirical cooperative game and assigns interaction-aware expert values via a Shapley-style attribution over observed top-$k$ coalitions, enabling the identification of experts that are essential for high-utility collaborations rather than merely frequent. To preserve MoE topology under a global pruning budget, SHAPE further introduces a \emph{quality-coverage} selection rule that retains, in each layer, the minimal expert subset covering an $α$ fraction of non-negative Shapley mass, while using bisection to match a target keep rate. Experiments on three modern MoE backbones (Qwen3-30B-A3B, GPT-OSS-20B, and DeepSeek-V2-Lite) across diverse benchmarks show that SHAPE consistently improves robustness over global and layer-wise pruning variants, maintaining competitive accuracy under 20\% and 40\% expert pruning without additional training and delivering clear reductions in peak GPU memory footprint. The open-source code is available at https://github.com/Alizen-1009/Shapley-Moe.

</details>


### [283] [SocraticPO: Policy Optimization via Interactive Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09887)
*Zirui Liu,Jie Ouyang,Qi Liu,Xianquan Wang,Jiayu Liu,Tingyue Pan,Qingchuan Li,Jing Sha,Zhenya Huang,Shijin Wang,Enhong Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Socratic Policy Optimization (SocraticPO), a reinforcement learning (RL) framework for language models that incorporates natural-language guidance and reward decay to improve reasoning performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The current approach of using scalar outcome rewards in RL for language models lacks the ability to guide models on correcting their reasoning mistakes, which can result in shortcut learning and less robust policies.

Method: SocraticPO introduces a teacher-student dynamic during RL rollouts. The student independently provides answers; if incorrect, the teacher gives concise, Socratic-style textual guidance to diagnose and improve the reasoning. Reward decay ensures the model doesn't overly rely on teacher intervention.

Result: SocraticPO outperforms strong RL and self-distillation baselines on undergraduate-level scientific reasoning benchmarks (e.g., SciKnowEval). It demonstrates the effectiveness of using both targeted guidance and reward decay.

Conclusion: The framework improves reasoning in language models by providing corrective guidance and discouraging dependency on teacher aid, and it can integrate into existing policy-gradient methods, leveraging even stronger black-box teacher models.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for large language models usually supervises reasoning with scalar outcome rewards, such as binary correctness. Such rewards provide an optimization direction but rarely explain how a model should revise its mistaken reasoning, which can encourage shortcut learning and brittle policies. We propose \textbf{SocraticPO} (Socratic Policy Optimization), a policy-optimization framework that augments RL rollouts with Socratic-style natural-language guidance. During rollout, the student first answers independently; if the answer is incorrect, a teacher diagnoses the attempt and provides concise corrective guidance, after which the student continues under the expanded context. Crucially, this guidance is paired with reward decay: correct answers obtained after teacher intervention only receive decayed rewards, preventing the policy from treating teacher help as a free path to reward. Since SocraticPO only modifies the rollout process while leaving the standard expected-reward objective intact, it can be plugged into existing policy-gradient backends such as Reinforce++. Moreover, because the teacher provides only text-level guidance, SocraticPO can leverage stronger black-box teacher models without requiring access to logits or distribution matching. On undergraduate-level scientific reasoning benchmarks from SciKnowEval, SocraticPO improves over strong RL and self-distillation baselines. Ablations show that both targeted guidance and reward decay are necessary, with reward decay mitigating reliance on assisted correction.

</details>


### [284] [SinkRec: Mitigating Semantic State Sink in Long Sequence Recommendation with Memory-Conditioned Gated Delta Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09888)
*Zhuang Zhuang,Zhipeng Wei,Ji Dai,Jie Chen,Fei Pan,Peng Jiang,Kun Gai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SinkRec proposes a new architecture for long-sequence recommendations, addressing the issue of semantic state sink in linear attention models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address inefficiencies in linear attention models caused by 'semantic state sink,' where repetitive patterns dominate and degrade the model's performance.

Method: SinkRec uses residual vector quantization to store repetitive patterns in an external memory. It also introduces a specialized model (TDGD) to refine memory operations, focusing the recurrent state on dynamic transitions.

Result: SinkRec mitigates the semantic state sink issue with a linear-time mechanism, showing effectiveness and efficiency on multiple datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed architecture, SinkRec, successfully decouples repetitive patterns from dynamic modeling, improving recommendation system performance while maintaining computational efficiency.

Abstract: Linear attention provides an efficient backbone for long-sequence recommendation by avoiding the quadratic cost of standard Transformers, but its compressed recurrent state can be dominated by repetitive behavior patterns. We identify this phenomenon as semantic state sink, where recurring semantics over-occupy the recurrent state and bias subsequent readouts. To mitigate semantic state sink, we propose SinkRec, a hybrid memory-transition looped architecture that decouples collaborative behavioral pattern storage from dynamic transition modeling. SinkRec externalizes recurring local patterns into a learnable conditional memory through residual vector quantization, reinjects the retrieved codes, and exposes memory key-value pairs to the attention block. It further introduces Temporal-Aware State-Relation Differential Gated DeltaNet (TDGD), which uses memory to purify recurrent writing and reading by suppressing memory-covered updates and removing memory-aligned readout responses. This design turns recurring semantics from state-competing signals into memory-retrievable patterns, allowing the recurrent state to focus on dynamic transitions and alleviating semantic state sink with linear-time efficiency. Experiments on public and industrial datasets demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of SinkRec.

</details>


### [285] [Optuna Constrained Tree-Structured Parzen Estimator Is a Joint Density Generalization of c-TPE](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09889)
*Shuhei Watanabe,Kaichi Irie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper analyzes Optuna's constrained TPE used in hyperparameter optimization (HPO) and compares it against an independent c-TPE approach, clarifying their differences and respective trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to uncover and analyze the underlying algorithmic principles of constrained TPE in Optuna since it is widely used but lacks proper theoretical examination.

Method: The authors compare two constrained TPE formulations (joint c-TPE and independent c-TPE), highlighting their acquisition functions and theoretical behaviors under specific conditions like constraint duplication.

Result: The analysis shows that joint c-TPE remains robust to duplicated constraints, whereas independent c-TPE’s performance degrades due to accumulating duplicate factors.

Conclusion: While joint c-TPE and independent c-TPE have distinct advantages and disadvantages, understanding their trade-offs can guide practitioners in selecting the appropriate method. Potential future research directions are also proposed.

Abstract: Constrained hyperparameter optimization (HPO) is common in practice, yet Optuna's widely used constrained TPE lacks algorithmic analysis. While c-TPE proposes an expected constrained improvement (ECI) approach assuming independence between the objective and constraints, Optuna uses a single joint density over both. We show that Optuna's constrained TPE is joint c-TPE -- the same ECI acquisition function using a joint likelihood. We demonstrate joint c-TPE is invariant to constraint duplication whereas independent c-TPE degrades as the product accumulates duplicated factors. We outline practical tradeoffs between the formulations and directions for future study.

</details>


### [286] [PreAct-Bench: Benchmarking Predictive Monitoring in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09890)
*Hainiu Xu,Italo Luis da Silva,Jiangnan Ye,Yuhao Wang,Wei Liu,Linyi Yang,Jonathan Richard Schwarz,Nicola Paoletti,Yulan He,Hanqi Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces 'Predictive Monitoring' to foresee unethical actions from partial action trajectories of LLMs and presents a benchmark, PreActBench, to facilitate this safety task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety research for LLMs is retrospective, identifying unethical behavior only after harm has occurred. This paper seeks to proactively identify unethical actions before they are fully executed.

Method: The authors develop PreActBench, a benchmark containing 1,000 paired ethical and unethical action trajectories across five domains. They evaluate various LLMs, safety guardrails, and probing methods using a custom metric called Prefix Foresight F1.

Result: Results reveal that humans perform better at predictive monitoring than current models, suggesting that advanced LLMs face significant challenges in this proactive safety task.

Conclusion: Proactive safety mechanisms for LLMs remain underdeveloped, and predictive monitoring offers a promising yet challenging avenue for future research in LLM risk reasoning.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents capable of executing multi-step action trajectories toward a given objective. While existing safety research has focused on detecting unethical behavior from complete trajectories, this paradigm is fundamentally retrospective: it identifies harm only after it has already occurred. In this work, we study a critical yet overlooked safety task, which we term Predictive Monitoring: given only a partial action trajectory, can a model infer whether it will culminate in an unethical action before the overt action is executed? To support this task, we present PreActBench, a benchmark of 1,000 paired ethical and unethical action trajectories spanning five domains. We evaluate a range of LLMs, safety guardrail models, and latent probing methods across varying fractions of the action trajectory using our Prefix Foresight F1 metric. Results show that while humans achieve promising performance, predictive monitoring remains challenging even for strong models, highlighting the need for future-oriented risk reasoning in LLM safety.

</details>


### [287] [Representation Curriculum: Stagewise Training for Robust Ranking and Allocation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09891)
*Ehsan Ebrahimzadeh,Sina Baharlouei,Abraham Bagherjeiran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses challenges in ranking systems of digital marketplaces that overly rely on exposure-dependent signals, proposing "Representation Curriculum" (RC) as a solution to rebalance reliance toward content-based merit signals for improved robustness and cold-start generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern ranking systems use exposure-dependent signals, which under stationary demand work well but tend to entrench existing items, degrade cold-start capabilities, and become less robust under changes.

Method: The proposed method, Representation Curriculum (RC), introduces exposure-dependent signals gradually during training while ensuring the model stays anchored to content-based signals, thereby curbing over-reliance on historical data.

Result: RC reduces population risk in cold-start scenarios, balances trade-offs between historical performance and new generalization, and shows measurable gains in experiments on learning-to-rank, recommendation benchmarks, and online e-commerce systems.

Conclusion: RC is effective in shifting reliance from exposure-driven signals to merit-based content, enhancing performance in cold-start populations while maintaining controlled trade-offs in established environments.

Abstract: Ranking in digital marketplaces is a dynamic exposure-allocation mechanism: displayed items shape discovery trajectories and success events logged by the platform to update future allocation policies. Modern ranking systems rely heavily on exposure-confounded signals (e.g. popularity estimates, CTR/CVR aggregates, and ID-based representation), because they are highly predictive under stationary demand. Yet this predictive power can become a learning shortcut: early access to exposure-dependent belief signals steers optimization toward over-reliance on them and away from exposure-independent merit signals (e.g., content-based competitiveness and semantic affinity). Consequently, the learned policy tends to entrench incumbents and degrade cold-start generalization and robustness under distribution shift. We propose Representation Curriculum (RC), a training-time intervention that temporally stages feature utilization. RC foregrounds content-based merit signals initially, then introduces exposure-dependent belief signals while anchoring the content pathway near the learned merit representation, curbing shortcut reliance on historical signals and mitigating gradient starvation on content signals. We formalize RC independently of task and hypothesis class and provide ranking-specific instantiations. In a Gaussian linear ridge setting, we derive closed-form solutions and sufficient conditions under which RC strictly reduces population risk on a cold-start target distribution, with a quantified Pareto tradeoff against source performance. Experiments on public learning-to-rank and recommendation benchmarks, and randomized online experiments in a large-scale e-commerce search system, show that RC measurably shifts reliance from historical belief signals toward content-based merit signals and yields consistent gains on cold populations with a controlled trade-off in head performance.

</details>


### [288] [LMT: A Bayesian Framework for Causal Discovery from Textual Alarm Records in Manufacturing Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09892)
*Xiaofeng Xiao,Jianhong Chen,Qiuzhuang Sun,Naichen Shi,Xubo Yue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces LMT, a Bayesian framework using LLMs and timestamps to discover causal relationships in alarm logs and event data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of identifying true causal relationships in engineering event data by combining textual descriptions and temporal evidence.

Method: The authors propose LMT, which uses large language models (LLMs) to extract semantic causal clues and combines them with timestamp evidence via a Bayesian approach.

Result: Simulation results demonstrate that LMT effectively discovers causality, especially in small sample scenarios.

Conclusion: LMT successfully integrates textual and temporal data to produce interpretable and accurate causal graphs for engineering application needs.

Abstract: Textual event records, such as alarm logs, have become an increasingly common data source in engineering and manufacturing systems. Beyond identifying correlations or recurring patterns, engineers are often interested in understanding which types of events causally trigger or influence other events during system operation. Textual event descriptions may contain semantic clues about such causal relationships, and recent large language models (LLMs) provide a promising tool for extracting these signals. However, relying solely on LLM-encoded textual information is insufficient for accurate causal discovery, since semantic patterns do not directly reveal causal mechanisms and may confuse causation with correlation or frequent sequential patterns. To address these challenges, we propose \textbf{LMT}, a Bayesian causal discovery framework for engineering event data that jointly leverages textual descriptions and timestamps. Specifically, LMT first uses LLMs to extract semantic causal signals from event descriptions and constructs a prior distribution over causal graphs among event types or event clusters. It then incorporates temporal evidence through a Poisson-process-based likelihood, allowing the LLM-informed prior to be refined by timestamp-based statistical evidence. By integrating the textual and temporal information, LMT produces a causal graph that is both interpretable and data-supported. Simulation studies show that the proposed framework is effective across different settings and is especially advantageous in small-sample alarm-event scenarios.

</details>


### [289] [A Navigable Manifold of Hypothesized Consciousness-Spectrum States in Language Model Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09894)
*Sophie Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether transformer language models possess a geometrically structured representation of human-like consciousness states. It finds such structure in the models' embedding spaces, which align with human consciousness spectrums, from reactive to integrative patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
