Method: The method formalizes action-sufficient compression, examines policy equivalence for defining optimal compression, introduces bounded expected policy regret to define approximate sufficiency, and uses a rate-regret analysis with a regret distortion metric.

Result: The optimal action channel is derived in the form of Gibbs distribution, tailored to preserve distinctions relevant to actions as dictated by consequence geometry while minimizing regret.

Conclusion: Preserving support details irrelevant to actions is unnecessary; instead, systems should focus on action-relevant distinctions for robust single-cycle decision-making.

Abstract: Robust decision-making requires compression. A system that forms a rich support state cannot usually preserve its full structure at the point of action. It must retain only those distinctions needed to act, verify, abstain, or defer under the current consequence geometry. This paper formalizes support sufficiency as action-sufficient compression. Let $H$ denote a full support state, $\mathcal{A}$ a finite action set, and $Z$ a consequence geometry specifying payoff structure. For fixed $Z$, the coarsest exactly action-sufficient compression is the quotient of support space by policy equivalence. Two support states may be merged exactly when they require the same optimal action. This clarifies why content-only and scalar-confidence-only arbitration fail whenever their induced partitions cross action boundaries. Approximate sufficiency is then defined by bounded expected policy regret. In the finite single-cycle setting, this yields a rate-regret problem with source $H$, reproduction alphabet $\mathcal{A}$, and distortion given by consequence-sensitive regret. The optimal stochastic action channel inherits the standard rate-distortion Gibbs form, applied here to support states with regret distortion. The contribution is interpretive: action adequacy is distinguished from reconstruction fidelity, information-bottleneck prediction, and rational inattention. Robust single-cycle arbitration does not require preserving all support, but it does require preserving the distinctions that consequence geometry makes action-relevant.

</details>


### [581] [The Bioelectrical Information Theory: Investigating the theoretical compression limit of bioelectrical signals under artificial intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09922)
*Jiawen Zou,Bo Yan*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper outlines a new framework for compressing bioelectrical signals by prioritizing information relevant to physiological structure and tasks, rather than raw waveform fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current brain-computer interfaces struggle to handle the bandwidth of bioelectrical signal acquisition. Existing compression techniques focus on waveform preservation, limited by entropy, and fail to optimize compression based on task relevance and physiological structure.

Method: The paper introduces an information-theoretic framework which includes a three-level hierarchy: (1) Signal Level – denoising to highlight physiological information, (2) Physiological Level – parametric encoding for compact representation, and (3) Semantic Level – task-specific compression using deep learning to replace marginal entropy with conditional entropy.

Result: The framework reconceptualizes the compression limits of bioelectrical signals, asserting that they are not fixed but adaptive to the capacity of models and downstream task requirements.

Conclusion: The proposed approach shifts the focus of bioelectrical compression from waveform transmission to transmitting residual task-relevant information, enabled by advanced models and physiological interfaces.

Abstract: Bioelectrical signals are increasingly acquired at scales that challenge the bandwidth of brain-computer interfaces. However, their compression is still often framed as a problem of waveform preservation, limited by the entropy of the raw signal. Here we propose an information-theoretic framework in which the effective information of bioelectrical data is determined not only by signal fidelity, but also by physiological structure, model capacity and downstream task requirements. We formulate bioelectrical compression as a three-level hierarchy. At the signal level, noise is reduced to the information they carry about latent physiological sources. At the physiological level, parametric encoders map purified signals into compact, structured and quantized representations. At the semantic level, task-irrelevant information is discarded, while deep learning models exploit causal dependencies to replace marginal entropy with conditional entropy. This perspective reframes the compression limit of bioelectrical signals as a model- and task-conditioned quantity rather than a fixed property of the waveform. As increasingly expressive models become integrated with neural and physiological interfaces, bioelectrical compression may shift from transmitting signals to transmitting only the residual information required for task-level interpretation.

</details>


### [582] [Minimum Distortion Quantization with Specified Output Distribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10458)
*Aolin Xu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper derives the optimal quantizer for a real-valued random variable, ensuring specified output distribution and minimized estimation error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To design efficient quantization methods that provide control over output distribution and ensure minimized estimation error (MMSE).

Method: The authors derive a mathematical formulation for the optimal quantizer using concepts like cumulative distribution functions (CDFs), optimal permutations, and majorization.

Result: The optimal quantizer has a specific form depending on the distribution properties, and the role of majorization is crucial for proving its effectiveness.

Conclusion: Specifying output distributions is essential for achieving objectives like controlled output entropy, improved communication channel performance, and data anonymization.

Abstract: We derive the optimal quantizer of a real-valued random variable $W$ with distribution $P_W$ such that 1) the distribution of the quantization output $X$ that can take $k$ values follows any specified distribution $P_X$ over $\{1,\ldots,k\}$, and 2) the minimum mean squared error (MMSE) of estimating $W$ from $X$ is minimized. It is shown that the optimal quantizer takes the form $X=σ\big(F_{σ^{-1}(X)}^{-1}(F_W(W))\big)$, where $σ$ is the optimal permutation of $\{1,\ldots,k\}$ among all permutations to minimize the MMSE, and $F$ is the cumulative distribution function. When $P_W$ is uniform over an interval or $P_X$ is uniform over $\{1,\ldots,k\}$, the quantizer takes a simple form $X=F_{X}^{-1}(F_W(W))$. The concept of majorization plays a key role in the optimality proof. Specifying the output distribution is useful for designing quantizers with explicitly controlled output entropy, maximized mutual information between input and output, tailored output distribution to match channel input requirements for communication, and data anonymization.

</details>


### [583] [Secure Aggregation with Top-K Sparsification in Decentralized Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10780)
*Hengxuan Tang,Jinbao Zhu,Xiaohu Tang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper presents a scheme for secure aggregation with top-K sparsification in decentralized federated learning to handle gradient leakage, achieving reduced communication cost while maintaining model accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address gradient leakage and high communication costs in federated learning, particularly for large models and decentralized systems with unreliable nodes.

Method: Proposes a sparse secure aggregation scheme using random masks, permutations, and an offline phase to efficiently handle top-K gradient entries while ensuring security under user dropouts and collusion.

Result: The proposed method reduced communication cost significantly and maintained accuracy comparable to full-gradient aggregation, even with just 1% gradient sparsification.

Conclusion: The scheme is an advanced solution for secure and efficient federated learning, effectively balancing communication efficiency and model accuracy.

Abstract: Secure aggregation is a vital component for mitigating gradient leakage in federated learning, but its communication cost conventionally scales with the gradient dimension. This becomes prohibitive for large models and even more pronounced in decentralized federated learning with limited bandwidth and unreliable nodes. Top-K gradient sparsification is an effective approach to reduce communication by transmitting only a few entries of the full gradient, while maintaining competitive model accuracy. Nevertheless, the top-K entries selected by each user are unpredictable and vary across users, which poses a challenge for efficient sparse secure aggregation. This paper studies information-theoretic secure aggregation with top-K sparsification in decentralized federated learning under user dropouts and user collusion. We propose a communication-efficient sparse secure aggregation scheme that offloads dimension-dependent overhead to an offline phase and protects private gradients using random masks and permutations. Experimental results demonstrate that our scheme preserves accuracy comparable to full-gradient aggregation even with only 1% gradient sparsification, while substantially reducing the communication cost.

</details>


<div id='hep-ph'></div>

# hep-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [584] [Efficient AI-Inspired Reduction of Feynman Integrals via Tube Seeding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10698)
*Justin Berman,Francois Charton,Andres Luna,Matthias Wilhelm,Mao Zeng*

Main category: hep-ph

TL;DR: The paper presents a machine learning-based seeding strategy for improving Feynman integral reduction, significantly reducing computational costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current state-of-the-art methods in theoretical particle physics and gravitational-wave calculations face bottlenecks due to the inefficiency in integration-by-parts reduction of Feynman integrals, particularly with increasing numerator powers and integral complexity.

Method: The authors employ machine learning to identify a sparse selection of seed integrals, enabling efficient reduction via the Laporta algorithm. The seeding follows a tube-like region connecting target integrals to master integrals along a zigzag path, avoiding conventional polynomial growth of seeds.

Result: The proposed method successfully reduces non-planar 2-loop 5-point integrals of rank 20 over finite fields and handles a complete set of top-level rank-10 integrals with significantly reduced time and memory usage compared to existing strategies.

Conclusion: This machine learning-driven sparse seeding approach is a feasible and efficient alternative for reducing Feynman integrals, with implementation available for practical use, paving the way for further advancements in computational physics.

Abstract: In this paper, we use machine learning to discover a new seeding strategy for integration-by-parts reduction of Feynman integrals, which is a frequent bottleneck in state-of-the-art calculations in theoretical particle and gravitational-wave physics. Our strategy allows us to reduce multi-loop integrals with large numerator powers via essentially the standard Laporta algorithm but with a sparse selection of seed integrals that grows only linearly with the numerator power, whereas existing strategies lead to growth with a polynomial power that increases with the complexity of the integral being reduced. The seeds are restricted to a thin tube-like region that connects the target integral to the master integrals along a zigzag path. We demonstrate the power of our approach by reducing non-planar 2-loop 5-point integrals of rank 20 with numerical kinematics over a finite field, which is prohibitively difficult for the Laporta algorithm with conventional seeding. Going beyond individual integrals, we further demonstrate the reduction of a complete set of top-level rank-10 integrals by dividing the target integrals into several chunks, each of which can be solved by our sparse seeding strategy with considerably less time and a significantly lower memory footprint than other state-of-the-art strategies, making the approach well-suited for phenomenological applications. We provide a proof-of-principle implementation on GitHub at https://github.com/andreslunagodoy/tube_seeding.

</details>


<div id='cs.CE'></div>

# cs.CE [[Back]](#toc)

### [585] [Non-linear mechanical field reconstruction coupling recurrent neural networks with physics-informed graph neural networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10909)
*Manuel Ricardo Guevara Garban,Yves Chemisky,Étienne Prulière,Michaël Clément,Martin Abendroth,Björn Kiefer*

Main category: cs.CE

TL;DR: The paper presents a coupled LSTM-GNN model for fast and accurate reconstruction of stress fields in heterogeneous microstructures under complex, history-dependent loading, achieving significant speedups and strong generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Simulations that reconstruct local stress fields under nonlinear, time-dependent loading are computationally expensive, creating a bottleneck in multi-scale simulations.

Method: The approach integrates LSTM for encoding macroscopic stress-strain sequences and GNN for spatial stress field reconstruction. A relative weighting strategy with linear warm-up addresses convergence issues in elasto-plastic regimes.

Result: The model is trained on 10,000 loading paths, achieving a 1000x speedup over finite element simulations while maintaining 1.9% cumulative error. It also generalizes to longer loading sequences and mesh variations without retraining.

Conclusion: The LSTM-GNN framework combines temporal and spatial modeling, providing accurate, efficient, and transferable stress field reconstructions suitable for diverse scenarios and mesh configurations.

Abstract: Reconstructing local stress fields in heterogeneous microstructures under non-linear, history-dependent loading remains a major computational bottleneck in multi-scale simulations. We propose a coupled LSTM-GNN framework that links the temporal and spatial aspects of local stress field reconstruction. A Long Short-Term Memory network encodes macroscopic stress-strain sequences into a compact hidden state that captures the path-dependent constitutive response, while a physics-informed Graph Neural Network reconstructs the spatially-resolved stress field at each time step. We introduce a relative weighting strategy with linear warm-up to balance the data-driven reconstruction loss and a discrete divergence-based equilibrium penalty. This resolves the scale mismatch that prevents fixed-weight formulations from converging in the elasto-plastic regime. The model is trained on 10,000 non-proportional loading paths applied to a periodic plate-with-a-hole microstructure and von Mises elasto-plasticity. The model achieves three orders of magnitude speedup over finite element simulations and generalizes to loading sequences twice the training length, with 1.9% cumulative error. Because the graph relies on mesh connectivity instead of the specific element type, one trained surrogate can be applied directly without retraining to meshes with different element types and to both coarser and finer resolutions, while in all cases reproducing the high-fidelity quad-element FE field used during training. Indeed, the message passing characteristics inherent to GNN and MeshGraphNet architecture render the model mesh-agnostic. Analysis of the LSTM hidden states suggests a low-dimensional structure related to the internal state variables of the constitutive model.

</details>


### [586] [A Constrained Natural-Language Interface for Variational Multi-Physics Finite Element Simulations in FEniCS](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10928)
*Nilay Upadhyay,Wesley F. Reinhart*

Main category: cs.CE

TL;DR: The paper proposes a constrained natural-language interface for finite element analysis using large language models while minimizing reliability risks by focusing the LLM only on front-end tasks like parsing prompts and geometry generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address reliability risks in finite element simulations caused by large language models writing solver code while reducing manual effort.

Method: The system restricts LLM involvement to front-end tasks such as prompt parsing into structured JSON and geometry creation while using deterministic templates for solver generation. Validation was done through analytical solutions and benchmarks.

Result: Parsing achieved a 100% valid final parse rate and field-extraction accuracy of 97.1%. The geometry generation benchmark saw a 90% first-pass and final success rate. The system effectively analyzed test cases, including a 3D elastoplastic L-bracket.

Conclusion: The constrained design ensures reliability and effectiveness in using natural language for variational finite element simulations without relying on autonomous code generation.

Abstract: Large language models can reduce the manual effort required to set up finite element simulations, but they introduce reliability risks when generated solver code lies on the critical path. We present a constrained natural-language interface for multi-physics finite element analysis in which the LLM is limited to front-end tasks: parsing prompts into structured JSON, generating Gmsh code only for non-catalog geometries, and using retry feedback for those stages. It never writes FEniCS solver templates, derives weak forms, or writes the numerical solver core. A deterministic dispatcher maps the validated specification to five human-written FEniCS/UFL templates: linear elasticity, hyperelasticity, elastoplasticity, thermo-mechanical coupling, and phase-field fracture. We validate this deterministic template layer against analytical solutions and published 2D/3D benchmarks. Smooth cases reach sub-percent agreement on adequate meshes, while harder nonlinear cases reach the 2-5 percent range. We also evaluate the LLM-facing front end directly. In a 15-prompt parser benchmark, first-pass valid parses were obtained for 9 cases, and all remaining cases were repaired after retry, giving a final valid parse rate of 100.0 percent, 100.0 percent problem-class accuracy, and 97.1 percent field-extraction accuracy. In a 10-case custom-geometry benchmark routed through the real LLM-to-Gmsh path, first-pass and final success were both 90.0 percent, with one unrecovered invalid-geometry failure. These results show that the parser and constrained prompt/validation design are effective on these benchmarks. As an end-to-end demonstration, the system generates and analyzes a 3D elastoplastic L-bracket with a fillet and bolt hole from one natural-language prompt. The contribution is a measured architecture for natural-language-driven variational simulation, not open-ended autonomous code generation.

</details>


<div id='astro-ph.GA'></div>

# astro-ph.GA [[Back]](#toc)

### [587] [Integral Field Unit Spectroscopy with One Fiber](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10197)
*Zehao Peng,Biprateep Dey,Chris J. Maddison,Joshua S. Speagle*

Main category: astro-ph.GA

TL;DR: The paper introduces a model predicting spatially resolved galaxy spectra from images without needing IFU data, achieving results comparable to models trained on IFU observations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Integral field spectroscopy provides detailed insights into galaxy evolution but is limited by observational constraints and dataset size.

Method: A probabilistic foundation model, leveraging a masked autoencoder framework with specialized encodings, predicts spatially resolved galaxy spectra using broadband images and single-fiber spectral data.

Result: Predicted emission line flux maps closely match independent IFU observations (MaNGA survey) and perform comparably to models trained on IFU data.

Conclusion: This approach demonstrates the potential to produce IFU-like spectra predictions with greater scalability, bypassing the limitations of traditional IFU-based observations.

Abstract: Integral field unit (IFU) spectroscopy provides spatially resolved spectra across galaxies, offering crucial insights into their evolution. However, its high observational cost limits current IFU datasets to $\sim 10^4$ objects. We present a multi-modal, probabilistic foundation model that predicts high-resolution spectra with calibrated uncertainties at arbitrary spatial locations within a galaxy directly from broadband images. Built on a masked autoencoder framework, our architecture injects fiber positional encodings and redshift aware wavelength encodings, enabling spatially conditioned predictions. Trained on 4.7 million images and single fiber spectroscopic observations from the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) survey, our model exploits the natural variance of fiber placements and the morphological self-similarity of galaxies to achieve IFU-like capabilities without any IFU training data. Predicted emission line flux maps match independent IFU observations from the Mapping Nearby Galaxies at APO (MaNGA) survey, with performance comparable to a supervised baseline trained directly on IFU data.

</details>


<div id='stat.ME'></div>

# stat.ME [[Back]](#toc)

### [588] [Deterministic Denominator Design for Localized Tamed Stochastic-Gradient Langevin Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10559)
*Yiwei Zhou,Ziheng Chen*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper proposes a deterministic approach to stabilize tamed stochastic-gradient Langevin dynamics (SGLD) by introducing a state-dependent envelope, avoiding randomness in denominator calculations and improving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tamed SGLD faces challenges due to instability caused by random denominators in its calculations. A reliable method is required to stabilize these dynamics while avoiding conditional mean drifts.

Method: The paper introduces a method involving a state-dependent envelope that is determined before the oracle sample is drawn. They design the envelope using a sequence: starting from an oracle score, creating a low-cost proxy score based on pilot states, setting thresholds via empirical quantiles, and applying a calibration layer. Analytical examination covers proxy errors, envelope errors, and resulting impact on SGLD performance.

Result: The proposed deterministic envelope method closely mimics the behavior of oracle scores while avoiding issues like random-denominator mean-shift. Experimental results demonstrate improved performance over simple deterministic taming techniques.

Conclusion: Deterministic denominators in SGLD, designed with structured proxy quantiles, provide a stable and effective alternative to random-denominator methods, enhancing stability and reliability in practice.

Abstract: Tamed stochastic-gradient Langevin dynamics (SGLD) stabilizes large drifts by adding a denominator to the update. If this denominator uses the same stochastic-gradient sample as the update step, it can also change the conditional mean drift. We study deterministic denominators: the state-dependent envelope is fixed before the current oracle sample is drawn. The main question is how to design this envelope in practice. The design starts from an oracle score, builds a low-cost proxy score on pilot states, chooses activation thresholds by empirical quantiles, and then applies a small calibration layer. The analysis tracks three steps: proxy and threshold errors become envelope errors; envelope errors perturb one SGLD step; and the local residuals give stationary errors through a conditional perturbation bridge. Experiments show that the proxy-quantile denominators are close to oracle-score behavior, avoid the random-denominator mean-shift channel, and improve simple deterministic taming choices.

</details>


### [589] [Correcting Variable Importance Scored by Random Forests](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10770)
*Guancheng Zhou,Haiping Xu,Jason Liu,Donghui Yan*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper discusses shortcomings in Random Forest (RF) variable importance due to variable correlations and proposes methods to account for conditional correlations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Variable importance in RF is widely used for tasks like model interpretation, but existing methodologies fail to address the effects of correlations among variables, which can distort importance indices.

Method: Two computational approaches are proposed. The first separates the variable of interest from correlated variables individually. The second uses clustering to group variables based on their pair-wise conditional correlations.

Result: The experiments demonstrate that these methods correct and improve the importance evaluation for variables, yielding more meaningful results.

Conclusion: Accounting for conditional correlations in calculating RF variable importance leads to improved and more accurate evaluation of variable significance.

Abstract: Variable importance produced by Random Forests (RF) is used widely in statistical data analysis, and has played an important role in a variety of tasks such as assisting model interpretation, model selection and diagnosis, and cost-bounded learning etc. However, the calculation of variable importance in RF does not take into account of the correlations among variables, and variables that are correlated to many other variables tend to receive a lower importance index or being completely masked (i.e., with an importance index near zero) by other strongly correlated variables. To prevent influence from unwanted correlated variables in calculating variable importance, we propose to group variables by their conditional correlations (conditional on the response variable). We explore two computationally efficient options, with one grouping variables individually, and then separates the variable of interest from all correlated variables, while the other uses clustering to group variables according to their pair-wise conditional correlations. Our experiments show that both lead to sensible corrections to the importance of variables.

</details>


<div id='cs.DB'></div>

# cs.DB [[Back]](#toc)

### [590] [Determination Provenance: From Ambiguity to Algebra](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10270)
*Joseph M. Hellerstein*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: The paper introduces determination provenance to handle multiple admissible outcomes in data systems, offering a more comprehensive framework than classical data provenance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing classical data provenance techniques cannot address situations where multiple outcomes are possible for the same input, such as in concurrent transactions or logic programs.

Method: The authors propose the concept of determination provenance, which uses supports (commutative semirings) and filtrations to track and measure semantic resolutions in data systems.

Result: The framework supports robust analysis and diagnostics of data resolutions and is instantiated for transactional isolation and $\mbox{Datalog}^\neg$, connecting semantic variants to the filtration framework.

Conclusion: Determination provenance enables a deeper understanding of ambiguous outcomes in data systems and facilitates compositional analysis and resolution diagnostics.

Abstract: Many data systems admit multiple admissible outcomes for the same input: concurrent transactions may serialize in one of many orders; a logic program may have multiple stable models. Classical data provenance cannot even pose its question in such settings -- it explains how a result was derived, but only after something has chosen which result to produce. We introduce \emph{determination provenance} to track the commitments that resolve this ambiguity. A tuple's \emph{support} is the set of resolutions under which it holds. Supports form a commutative semiring, and layered commitments induce a \emph{filtration} measuring each tuple's \emph{query-relative depth} -- how many layers of semantic resolution it depends on. Positive relational algebra respects the filtration, enabling compositional robustness analysis and quantitative diagnosis of resolution cost. We instantiate the framework for transactional isolation and for $\mbox{Datalog}^\neg$; in both, classical semantic variants (isolation levels; negation semantics) correspond to different views of a single shared filtration.

</details>


### [591] [Provenance Tracking in AI Compilers through the Lens of Coalgebra](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10937)
*Zilu Tian,Liying Liu*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: This paper introduces a lightweight method for tracking provenance in AI compilers using observational semantics, instead of invasive identifier propagation. A prototype, COVAN, demonstrates its effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provenance tracking in AI compilers is critical for tasks like debugging, postprocessing, and validating transformations. Existing methods are limited by complexity or inability to handle non-injective graph rewrites effectively.

Method: The authors propose using a generative approach based on observational semantics and formalize it through a coalgebraic model and bisimulation. Provenance is tracked by observing graph transformations rather than directly propagating identifiers.

Result: A prototype AI compiler, COVAN, was implemented to validate this approach, showing stable provenance tracking throughout compilation pipelines with minimal engineering complexity.

Conclusion: The proposed observational semantics-based method provides a reliable and minimally invasive solution for provenance tracking in AI compilers, outperforming traditional approaches in complex rewrite scenarios.

Abstract: AI compilers aggressively rewrite computation graphs through normalization, lowering, and optimization, making it difficult to track the provenance of tensors and operators across compilation. Reliable provenance is essential for attaching platform-specific postprocessing, debugging compiler behavior, and validating transformations, yet existing solutions are either invasive or ad hoc under non-injective graph rewrites.
  We present a lightweight, generative approach to provenance tracking based on observational semantics. Instead of propagating identifiers through compiler passes, we observe graph transformations and reason about provenance in terms of observable computational actions. We formalize this approach using a coalgebraic model and bisimulation, which preserves provenance even when intermediate nodes are eliminated. Furthermore, we implement this approach in a prototype AI compiler COVAN, demonstrating stable provenance across compilation pipelines with minimal engineering overhead.

</details>


<div id='cs.SD'></div>

# cs.SD [[Back]](#toc)

### [592] [SpeechJBB: Probing Safety Alignment and Comprehension in Large Audio Language Models under Code-Switched Speech](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06037)
*Virginia Ceccatelli,Yejin Jeon,David Ifeoluwa Adelani*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper evaluates the safety alignment of large audio language models (LALMs) under multilingual and spoken conditions, emphasizing code-switched speech, using a new dataset, SpeechJBB.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of LALMs' safety alignment are mostly monolingual and text-based, overlooking multilingual, spoken, and code-switched contexts, which limits understanding of their generalizability.

Method: The authors introduced SpeechJBB, an audio jailbreak dataset, and tested safety weaknesses under multilingual and code-switched conditions, including obfuscation using phonologically plausible pseudo-words.

Result: The study found high jailbreak success rates for code-switched harmful audio, especially in non-English contexts. Pseudo-word insertion further reduced refusal rates, enabling bypassing of safety policies.

Conclusion: LALMs exhibit significant safety vulnerabilities in multilingual, code-switched, and obfuscated speech settings, suggesting the need for improved safety mechanisms in such environments.

Abstract: Large audio language models (LALMs) are increasingly deployed in real-world applications, yet their safety alignment is still primarily evaluated on monolingual, text-based harmful prompts. This leaves their generalizability under multilingual and spoken settings, particularly code-switched speech, largely underexplored. To address this gap, we introduce SpeechJBB, an audio jailbreak dataset for benchmarking across multiple state-of-the-art LALMs. The extent of safety weaknesses is further probed by introducing an augmented setting where phonologically plausible pseudo-words are inserted around safety-critical terms to simulate localized obfuscation. Across models, code-switched harmful audio yields substantially high jailbreak success rates (JSR), with non-English monolingual and non-English code-switched pairs exhibiting the highest attack success. Pseudo-word insertion further reduces refusal rates, which demonstrates that natural-sounding obfuscation can effectively bypass safety policies.

</details>


### [593] [Enhancing Multilingual LLM-based ASR with Mixture of Experts and Dynamic Downsampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10439)
*Guodong Lin,Ziqi Chen,Yuxiang Fu,Ke Li,Wei-Qiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper introduces a projector-based LLM-ASR framework using Mixture of Experts and Continuous Integrate-and-Fire mechanisms to enhance multilingual adaptability and alignment, achieving significant performance gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address challenges in integrating large language models for automatic speech recognition, especially focusing on multilingual generalization and modality alignment.

Method: The method involves using a Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture for cross-lingual adaptability and a Continuous Integrate-and-Fire (CIF) mechanism for modality alignment.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate that the methodology significantly outperforms strong baseline models in ASR tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed approach makes progress toward developing more accurate, adaptable, and robust LLM-based ASR systems.

Abstract: The rapid progress of large language models (LLMs) has opened up a new frontier for automatic speech recognition (ASR), making their effective integration a critical and challenging research direction. To this end, this work proposes a projector-based LLM-ASR framework targeting the key challenges of multilingual generalization and modality alignment. Our approach incorporates a Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture to improve cross-lingual adaptability, and a Continuous Integrate-and-Fire (CIF) mechanism for dynamic downsampling and modality alignment. Experimental results show that the combination of these components yields substantial performance improvements, surpassing strong baseline models. The proposed method represents a step toward building more accurate, robust, and generalizable LLM-based ASR systems.

</details>


### [594] [Dual-Branch Gated Fusion for Open-Set Audio Deepfake Source Tracing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10223)
*Awais Khan,Kutub Uddin,Khalid Malik*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: This paper introduces a dual-branch gated fusion framework combining XLSR-53 and CORES features to improve the robustness and generalization of synthetic speech attribution under distribution shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current models for attributing synthetic utterances struggle with unseen (out-of-distribution) systems and often yield overconfident predictions, highlighting the need for a more robust and generalizable approach.

Method: The method pairs XLSR-53 features with a novel 66-dimensional descriptor (CORES) for capturing complementary synthesis artifacts. An input-conditioned gate adaptively balances the contributions of both features during joint training, using cross-entropy loss, energy margin loss, and a gate diversity term.

Result: The proposed system achieves high performance metrics on the MLAAD benchmark, including a 97.6% in-domain accuracy, 4.9% equal error rate (EERc), and an 83.5% relative reduction in false positive rate at 95% recall compared to the Interspeech 2025 baseline.

Conclusion: By effectively balancing SSL-derived features (XLSR-53) and hand-engineered attributes (CORES) through a gated fusion framework, the proposed approach significantly enhances synthetic speech attribution, particularly under challenging out-of-distribution scenarios.

Abstract: Attributing a synthetic utterance to its originating system remains an open challenge: closed-set models fail to reject unseen synthesizers and produce overconfident predictions. To address this, we propose a dual-branch gated fusion framework that pairs XLSR-53 with CORES, a 66-dimensional descriptor that, unlike prior Linear Filter Bank (LFB)-only work, spans cepstral, oscillatory, rhythmic, energy, and spectral dimensions to capture complementary synthesis artifacts. Our analysis shows XLSR-53 remains discriminative in-domain (ID) while CORES generalizes stably under distribution shift (OOD), yet their naive concatenation fails due to SSL representational imbalance. To resolve this, an input-conditioned gate adaptively weights each branch under joint training with cross-entropy, an energy margin loss for ID/OOD separation, and a gate diversity term. On the MLAAD benchmark, our system achieves 97.6\% ID accuracy, 4.9\% EERc, and an 83.5\% relative FPR95 reduction over the Interspeech 2025 baseline.

</details>


### [595] [Time-frequency localization of bird calls in dense soundscapes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10407)
*Simen Hexeberg,Fanghui Tong,Hari Vishnu,Mandar Chitre*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper frames bird call detection as an object detection challenge, using YOLO models to achieve higher accuracy in vocalization localization on spectrograms compared to baseline methods, and introduces new tools and metrics for evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the precision of localizing bird vocalizations in time-frequency within dense and complex acoustic environments, surpassing the limitations of existing bioacoustic classifiers.

Method: The study trains YOLO-based object detection models on spectrograms to detect and localize bird calls, introduces a browser-based annotation tool, and proposes a novel evaluation metric, Intersection over Minimum (IoMin), to handle ambiguous boundaries better.

Result: The best YOLO model achieved significantly improved performance, more than doubling the baseline in Singaporean soundscapes (81.8% vs. 42.1% IoMin@50 F1-score) and showing robust performance on out-of-distribution Hawaiian recordings as well.

Conclusion: Object detection techniques like YOLO offer a promising solution for accurately time-frequency localizing animal vocalizations in complex soundscapes, with improved metrics and tools enhancing their applicability.

Abstract: Passive acoustic monitoring enables large-scale observation of wildlife, but most bioacoustic classifiers only predict species presence in a time window without localizing vocalizations precisely in time or frequency, limiting downstream analyses. We formulate bird vocalization detection as an object detection task on spectrograms and train YOLO11 models to localize bird calls in dense tropical soundscapes from Singapore. We additionally introduce an open-source browser-based annotation tool and propose Intersection over Minimum (IoMin), an evaluation metric that better handles ambiguous acoustic boundaries than standard IoU and is better suited to the problem at hand. The best YOLO model nearly doubles baseline performance on in-distribution soundscapes from Singapore (81.8% vs. 42.1% IoMin@50 F1-score) while still outperforming the baseline on unseen out-of-distribution recordings from Hawaii (58.6% vs. 48.6%). These results suggest that object detection frameworks are a promising approach to time-frequency localization of animal vocalizations in complex soundscapes.

</details>


### [596] [Inside the Latent Flow: Causal Deciphering of Attention Dynamics in Audio Separation Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10046)
*Yuxuan Chen,Haoyuan Xu,Peize He*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method to accelerate audio separation in flow-matching transformers while maintaining high-quality results through insights into their attention dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the inefficiency and opaqueness of attention dynamics in flow-matching transformers used for audio separation.

Method: The authors adapt causal-intervention principles into a deterministic probing protocol to uncover attention mechanisms and propose a training-free optimization method called Layer-Selective Attention Caching (LSAC).

Result: LSAC reduces self-attention computation by about 25% while maintaining negligible loss in separation quality, achieving up to 6.7x better quality retention compared to naive step reduction.

Conclusion: Understanding internal attention mechanisms leads to practical optimizations, showcasing the alignment of theory with application-driven performance improvements in audio separation tasks.

Abstract: Flow-matching transformers achieve strong audio separation, yet their attention dynamics are opaque. We adapt established causal-intervention principles into a deterministic, inference-time probing protocol for SAM Audio. Orthogonal probing uncovers a dual-pathway text-conditioning mechanism: additive injections control semantic identity, while cross-attention refines acoustic structure. We observe an asynchronous layerwise convergence: stable layers build temporal scaffolds early, whereas fast layers continue resolving artifacts during sampling. The model also attenuates temporal segmentation cues to maintain continuous-flow stability. Using these insights, we propose Layer-Selective Attention Caching (LSAC), a training-free acceleration method that caches attention in stable layers. Across acoustic complexities, LSAC cuts self-attention computation by about ~25% with negligible quality loss and yields up to 6.7x higher quality retention than naive step reduction.

</details>


### [597] [Automated Pronunciation Evaluation for Korean Toddler Speech using Speech Diarization and Self-Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10213)
*Diane Myung-kyung Woodbridge,Jee Hyun Suh*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: This paper develops an automated pronunciation evaluation system for Korean toddler speech using speaker diarization and self-supervised learning, achieving high accuracy in experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The underdevelopment of automated pronunciation assessment tools for Korean toddlers, despite the prevalence of speech sound disorders, motivates this study to bridge the gap.

Method: The paper combines a neural speaker diarization model called NeMo SortFormer with self-supervised learning backbones for pronunciation scoring. Experiments are conducted on a novel IRB-approved Korean toddler speech corpus.

Result: NeMo SortFormer achieves 88.69% speaker count accuracy and 33.04% diarization error rate. For pronunciation, a model ensemble reaches consonant and vowel prediction accuracies of 0.720 and 0.845, respectively.

Conclusion: The proposed pipeline demonstrates strong performance for automated Korean toddler speech assessment, addressing gaps in existing tools and showcasing scalable diagnostic potential.

Abstract: Speech sound disorders affect approximately 44% of Korean pediatric communication disorder cases, yet automated assessment tools for Korean toddler speech remain underdeveloped. This paper presents an end-to-end pipeline for automated pronunciation evaluation of Korean toddler speech, combining neural speaker diarization with self-supervised speech representation learning. We introduce a novel IRB-approved corpus of 53 recordings from Korean-speaking children aged 2-5 years. A subset of 53 subjects was annotated by three independent reviewers, yielding 1,190 consonant and 748 vowel word-level binary correctness labels. We evaluate three diarization models, finding that NeMo SortFormer achieves 88.69% speaker count accuracy and 33.04% diarization error rate (DER) owing to its arrival-time-sorted transformer architecture, which handles the acoustic confound between young female caregivers exhibiting aegyo and toddler speech. For pronunciation scoring, we compare three self-supervised learning (SSL) backbones across multiple pooling strategies. A cross-model ensemble routing consonant prediction to HuBERT-large and vowel prediction to WavLM-large achieves balanced accuracies of 0.720 and 0.845, with a mean of 0.782.

</details>


### [598] [Linguistically Augmented Audio Speech Data (LinguAS)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10246)
*Ashley R. Keaton,Zahra Khanjani,Christine Mallinson,Vandana P. Janeja*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: This paper presents a dataset, LinguAS, which includes genuine and deepfaked audio samples annotated with linguistic features to improve speech deepfake detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the limitation of existing fake speech detection models, which largely rely on frame-level audio features and neglect linguistic cues characteristic of natural human speech.

Method: The authors introduced LinguAS, a dataset of over 800 genuine and deepfaked audio samples annotated with five expert-defined linguistic features, and metadata on speaker gender and spoofed audio generators.

Result: Models trained on LinguAS dataset, with its linguistic and metadata augmentations, outperformed baselines like ASVspoof 2021 and SSL models (e.g., HuBert, XLSR) in detecting deepfake speech.

Conclusion: Augmenting datasets with linguistic features and relevant metadata enhances the performance of fake speech detection models, providing more robust tools against deepfake audio.

Abstract: Maliciously-created fake speech, including deepfaked and spoofed audio, is proliferating at an alarming rate, and detection models are racing to stay ahead of the curve. Yet, most detection models are trained to make inference on frame-level audio features alone without leveraging valuable linguistic cues at larger timescales. To address this gap, we present Linguistically Augmented Audio Speech Data (LinguAS), a dataset of genuine and deepfaked audio samples annotated with five strategically-chosen, Expert-Defined Linguistic Features (EDLFs) that occur frequently in spoken English and are characteristic of natural human speech. LinguAS contains over 800 audio samples, each of which are annotated with EDLFs. The dataset has a balanced number of four spoofed audio attack types and a proportionate number of genuine speech samples. We also include metadata on speaker gender and the generator/source for each spoofed audio sample, offering more granularity for model training. We found that models trained on data augmented with EDLFs had improved model performance significantly beyond the ASVspoof 2021 deep learning baselines and SSL models like HuBert and XLSR. LinguAS's augmented linguistic, gender, and generator metadata provide audio deepfake researchers with a dataset that emphasizes real human language traits to improve model inference of faked speech. Data and code are publicly available.

</details>


### [599] [Towards Robust Arabic Speech Emotion Recognition with Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10278)
*Youcef Soufiane Gheffari,Samiya Silarbi*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The study focuses on improving Arabic Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) through hybrid architectures and finds CNN-Transformer to outperform other models with 98.1% accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in Arabic SER, such as dialectal diversity, limited datasets, and difficulties in modeling spectral and temporal dependencies.

Method: Three architectures were evaluated: CNN-LSTM, CNN-Transformer (using MFCC and spectrogram representations), and fine-tuned wav2vec 2.0 (operating on raw audio with self-supervised learning).

Result: The CNN-Transformer model achieved a 98.1% accuracy on the EYASE and BAVED datasets, outperforming other evaluated models.

Conclusion: Hybrid architectures, particularly CNN-Transformer, effectively improve Arabic SER, showcasing their ability to model both spectral and long-range dependencies in resource-constrained and diverse settings.

Abstract: Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) aims to identify a speaker's emotional state from audio signals. While recent advances in deep learning have significantly improved SER performance in Indo-European languages, Arabic SER remains underexplored and challenging due to dialectal diversity, limited annotated datasets, and the difficulty of modeling both local spectral cues and long-range temporal dependencies.
  To address these limitations, this study investigates whether hybrid architectures that jointly model spatial and contextual information can improve emotion recognition in Arabic speech. We propose and evaluate a comparative framework involving three architectures: a CNN-LSTM model, a CNN-Transformer model, and a fine-tuned wav2vec 2.0 model. The first two models leverage MFCC and spectrogram-based representations, while wav2vec 2.0 operates directly on raw audio through self-supervised representations.
  Experiments conducted on the EYASE and BAVED datasets demonstrate that the proposed CNN-Transformer architecture significantly outperforms the other models, achieving an accuracy of 98.1 percent. This result highlights the effectiveness of combining convolutional feature extraction with Transformer-based global context modeling.
  The main contribution of this work lies in providing a systematic comparison of hybrid and self-supervised approaches for Arabic SER, and in demonstrating that CNN-Transformer architectures offer a robust solution for capturing both spectral and long-range dependencies in low-resource and dialectally diverse settings.

</details>


### [600] [Speech Meets ELF: Audio Conditional Continuous-Target Diffusion for Speech Recognition and Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10368)
*Xuanchen Li,Tianrui Wang,Yuheng Lu,Zikang Huang,Yu Jiang,Chenghan Lin,Chenrui Cui,Ziyang Ma,Xingyu Ma,Chunyu Qiang,Guochen Yu,Xie Chen,Longbiao Wang,Jianwu Dang*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper introduces ELF-S2T, a novel audio-conditioned continuous-target generative model for speech-to-text (S2T) tasks, improving performance in ASR and S2TT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore continuous-target language modeling for S2T tasks, which remains unexplored in comparison to standard discrete text token generation approaches.

Method: The proposed ELF-S2T leverages pre-trained Embedded Language Flows and processes speech using a frozen Whisper encoder. It prepends the audio condition to noisy latent text for flow-matching denoising, applies audio forcing during training, and uses classifier-free guidance during inference.

Result: ELF-S2T demonstrates competitive performance in ASR and S2TT on LibriSpeech and CoVoST2 datasets. Detailed error analysis ties ASR and S2TT errors to underlying continuous latent space confusions.

Conclusion: ELF-S2T bridges recognition and translation through semantic mapping in continuous latent space, uncovering commonalities between ASR and S2TT tasks while achieving competitive results.

Abstract: Speech-to-text (S2T) systems for recognition (ASR) and translation (S2TT) typically generate discrete text tokens. In contrast, continuous-target language modelling performs generation in a continuous space, yet its potential for S2T remains unexplored. To bridge this gap, we propose ELF-S2T, an audio-conditioned continuous-target generative model for S2T. Built upon the pre-trained Embedded Language Flows (ELF) backbone, ELF-S2T processes speech via a frozen Whisper encoder and a single linear projector, prepending the resulting audio condition to the noisy text latent for in-context, flow-matching denoising. To prevent the model from over-relying on its pre-trained text context, we introduce audio forcing during training, and further amplify the audio condition via classifier-free guidance at inference. Experiments on LibriSpeech and CoVoST2 show that ELF-S2T achieves competitive ASR and S2TT performance. Crucially, our error analysis reveals that, although ASR and S2TT errors look very different on the surface, both stem from the same underlying cause, a close distance confusion in the continuous latent space. This finding naturally aligns with the continuous representation generation paradigm, indicating a common semantic mapping process beneath recognition and translation. Our code and pretrained models are publicly available at https://github.com/Sslnon/ELF-S2T.

</details>


### [601] [RAT: Reference-Augmented Training for ASV Anti-Spoofing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10908)
*Vojtěch Staněk,Anton Firc,Jakub Reš,Kamil Malinka*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel method, Reference-Augmented Training (RAT), to improve deepfake detection by leveraging speaker-reference recordings during training, even if the reference is ignored during inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve spoofing countermeasure systems and enhance their robustness to deepfake attacks, especially when challenges like absent or mismatched references occur during inference.

Method: The authors propose Reference-Augmented Training (RAT), which involves training a spoofing detection system conditioned on speaker-reference recordings to induce invariance, even when references are not used during inference.

Result: RAT significantly improves detection performance, achieving state-of-the-art results on the ASVspoof 5 benchmark with a single detector (2.57% EER and 0.074 minDCF), outperforming large ensemble systems.

Conclusion: The optimization process diminishes reliance on the reference during inference, and RAT remains effective under various conditions, showcasing potential for robust and practical deepfake detection.

Abstract: We introduce a spoofing countermeasure architecture conditioned on speaker-reference recordings, but observe that it converges to a solution that effectively ignores the reference during inference. Surprisingly, training with a reference channel induces invariance that improves deepfake detection, even when the reference is absent or mismatched during inference. Based on this observation, we propose a Reference-Augmented Training (RAT) strategy. RAT yields improved detection performance compared to single-utterance baselines, even when the reference recording is replaced with a zero vector at inference. Through rigorous analysis, we demonstrate that the optimization process rapidly diminishes the reference contributions, leading to inference largely independent of the reference channel. Using RAT, we achieve state-of-the-art 2.57% EER and 0.074 minDCF on the ASVspoof 5 benchmark with a single detector, surpassing even large ensemble systems.

</details>


### [602] [Ethical and Technical Limits of Deepfake Speech Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10911)
*Vojtěch Staněk,Eva Trnovská,Kamil Malinka,Anton Firc*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper audits 39 deepfake speech datasets, highlighting issues with demographic metadata and overlapping source corpora that undermine fairness and generalization assessments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate the credibility and robustness of deepfake speech detection systems, given concerns about the adequacy of datasets used for training and evaluation.

Method: The study conducts a detailed audit of 39 deepfake speech datasets, analyzing attributes such as accessibility, documentation, demographics, language coverage, scale, and source corpora.

Result: Findings show that most datasets lack demographic metadata, hindering fairness assessments. Additionally, overlapping bona fide source corpora across datasets compromise cross-dataset evaluations.

Conclusion: Better dataset design and documentation are necessary to ensure robust fairness assessments and reliable generalization claims in deepfake speech detectors.

Abstract: Claims about the robustness and fairness of deepfake speech detectors are only as credible as the datasets used to train and evaluate those systems. We present a dataset-level audit of the deepfake speech landscape. We compile and analyze 39 deepfake speech datasets, examining key attributes including accessibility, documentation, demographic and language coverage, dataset scale, and the underlying bona fide speech sources. Our audit reveals two important takeaways. Firstly, fairness assessment is largely infeasible because most datasets lack demographic metadata, and only a few contain gender or language labels. This prevents any meaningful subgroup analysis and leaves other demographic attributes unaddressed. Secondly, we identify substantial overlap in underlying bona fide source corpora across datasets, which can undermine cross-dataset evaluation and lead to overstated generalization claims.

</details>


### [603] [What Do Deepfake Speech Detectors Actually Hear?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10912)
*Vojtěch Staněk,Veronika Jirmusová,Anton Firc,Kamil Malinka,Jakub Reš,Martin Perešíni*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper introduces an explainability method for deepfake speech detectors, using Integrated Gradients and self-supervised audio representations to localize evidence in the signal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deepfake speech detectors typically lack mechanisms to explain their decisions, leaving uncertainty about the cues they rely on.

Method: The authors employ Integrated Gradients on self-supervised audio representations to identify and annotate key signal regions impacting detector decisions.

Result: The study finds that different detectors emphasize varying cues like environmental sounds, phoneme artifacts, and word boundaries, validated by causal masking tests.

Conclusion: This work enhances transparency and understanding of deepfake speech detectors, showing that they utilize diverse signal features for their decisions.

Abstract: Deepfake speech detectors often output a single score without explaining why an audio sample is flagged, where in the signal the evidence lies, or what cues drive the decision. We propose an audio-native explainability pipeline using Integrated Gradients on time-aligned self-supervised representations to localize decision evidence over time. We apply the proposed method to three WavLM-based detectors (AASIST, CA-MHFA, SLS) on ASVspoof 5 and manually annotate the highest-attribution regions to provide a semantic meaning of the most important cues. Despite similar performance, the detectors rely on different cues: AASIST emphasizes non-speech/environment cues, CA-MHFA focuses on localized phoneme artifacts, and SLS relies on word boundaries and spectral integrity. We move beyond speculative reasoning and validate our findings by causal masking of the primary detector cues. Observed performance degradation further supports the explained detector semantics.

</details>


<div id='math.ST'></div>

# math.ST [[Back]](#toc)

### [604] [Intrinsic Footpoint-invariant Riemannian Cross-covariance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10212)
*Carlos Soto,Cheng Wang,Zipan Huang,Xiaoyu Chen*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method for defining and analyzing covariance for data residing on Riemannian manifolds, beyond traditional Euclidean spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Covariance estimation is fundamental in many learning tasks, but existing methods focus on Euclidean spaces and are inapplicable to nonlinear Riemannian manifolds, which are increasingly important in modern machine learning.

Method: The authors develop an intrinsic Riemannian cross-covariance by transporting variations to a common tangent space via parallel transport, preserving independence from arbitrary coordinate systems.

Result: The proposed covariance retains properties of Euclidean covariance, has desirable asymptotic behaviors, and is validated through numerical and real-data experiments on manifolds like spheres, SPD matrices, and Kendall's shape space.

Conclusion: Riemannian covariance is established as a versatile and fundamental tool for second-order learning and analysis in non-Euclidean data spaces.

Abstract: Covariance estimation yields a fundamental second-order statistic underlying representation learning, dimension reduction, and dependence modeling. While covariance has been well understood in Euclidean spaces, it is ill-defined for random objects residing on nonlinear Riemannian manifolds, which increasingly arise in modern machine learning applications involving shapes, symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrices, etc. This paper introduces an intrinsic Riemannian cross-covariance for manifold-valued random objects. Our approach defines covariance and correlation by transporting local variations to a common tangent space via parallel transport, yielding a second-order descriptor that is independent of arbitrary coordinate choices. We establish that the proposed covariance inherits desirable properties of its Euclidean counterparts and characterize its asymptotic behavior. Numerical studies on spheres and SPD manifolds, together with real-data experiments on heart valve shapes in Kendall's shape space, demonstrate the effectiveness of our estimators and verify the stated properties. Our results position the Riemannian covariance as a fundamental tool for second-order learning and analysis in non-Euclidean representation spaces.

</details>


### [605] [Conformal Prediction for Dyadic Regression Under Complex Missingness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11136)
*Robert Lunde,Minjie Yang,Elizaveta Levina,Ji Zhu*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: This paper develops a framework for conformal prediction in dyadic regression under complex missing data scenarios and establishes theoretical guarantees, while proposing novel methods and validating them on synthetic and real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in conformal prediction for dyadic data, specifically under complex missing patterns and weak distributional assumptions.

Method: A theoretical framework is established using weaker invariance conditions, novel bijection arguments, and new methods such as row-column approaches and graphon-weighted conformal procedures. Conditional validity for continuous and discrete responses is also proved.

Result: The paper achieves super-uniformity guarantees, asymptotic validity under certain nonparametric models, and conditional validity even under missing-not-at-random scenarios.

Conclusion: The methods are theoretically rigorous and practically applicable, with illustrations on synthetic and real network data, expanding conformal prediction theory to new classes of problems.

Abstract: We develop a framework for conformal prediction in dyadic regression problems under complex missingness mechanisms. At the theoretical level, we establish super-uniformity of conformal prediction under distributional invariance conditions weaker than exchangeability. A key result handles the case where the sample itself is a random subset of the index set, a setting not covered by existing theory, via a novel bijection argument that constructs an explicit measure-preserving correspondence between events. In addition, we propose conformal prediction procedures for jointly exchangeable arrays, including full conformal, split conformal, a row-column approach exploiting similarities within rows and columns, and a selective conformal procedure achieving mask-conditional validity. For missing elements, we establish asymptotic validity of a graphon-weighted conformal procedure under a nonparametric graphon model for the missingness mechanism. We further establish conditional validity results for both continuous and discrete responses; to the best of our knowledge, this is first formal proof of asymptotic conditional validity for weighted conformal prediction under a missing-not-at-random assumption. The proposed methods are illustrated on synthetic and real network data.

</details>


### [606] [Bidirectional Random Projections](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10377)
*Chao Lan,Luyuan Yang*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: The paper studies bidirectional random projections in OLS regression and establishes an excess loss bound for modified estimators, supported by numerical tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the implications of bidirectional random projections on the performance of OLS regression estimators in fixed-design settings.

Method: The authors use mathematical analysis to derive an expected excess loss bound and compare it to an established bound for unidirectional random projections. They also conduct numerical experiments on real-world datasets.

Result: The study reveals that the gap in performance measures scales as $O(p_1 + C \frac{1}{p_1})$, with $C$ depending on $n_1/n$ and even allowing for negative values under certain conditions.

Conclusion: Bidirectional random projections can be advantageous under specific configurations, as shown by the excess loss bound analysis and corroborated numerical results.

Abstract: This paper analyzes bidirectional random projections for ordinary least squares (OLS) regression under the fixed design setting. Let $(X,Y) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p} \times \mathbb{R}^n$ be a sample and $R \in \mathbb{R}^{n_1 \times n}, W \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times p_1}$ be two properly distributed random projections. We develop an expected excess loss bound for the OLS estimator built on $(WXR, WY)$. Compared to an established bound for OLS estimator built on $(XR, Y)$, the gap is approximately $O\left( p_1 + C \frac{1}{p_1} \right)$, where $C$ scales with $n_1/n$ and can be negative for small $n_1/n$. Its implications are confirmed by numerical results on real-world data.

</details>


<div id='physics.comp-ph'></div>

# physics.comp-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [607] [An adaptive framework for the axisymmetric pulsar magnetosphere using physics-informed Kolmogorov-Arnold networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10686)
*Spyros Rigas,Ioannis Contopoulos,Georgios Alexandridis,Antonios Nathanail*

Main category: physics.comp-ph

TL;DR: This paper refines the application of Physics-Informed Neural Networks for pulsar magnetosphere modeling by improving accuracy and efficiency while addressing prior limitations of manual tuning and convergence problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the inefficiencies and limitations of existing approaches for modeling pulsar magnetospheres, such as extensive manual calibration, long training times, and limited accuracy.

Method: The authors introduced domain-specific neural architectures based on Kolmogorov-Arnold networks, an adaptive training pipeline, and a physics-based convergence criterion for more automated and accurate modeling.

Result: The refined framework achieved mean squared errors at O(1e-6), reduced training time to under 20 minutes, and handled larger spatial scale disparities effectively. A correction to an important flux-equation relationship was also provided.

Conclusion: The proposed method significantly improves magnetosphere modeling accuracy and efficiency, overcomes challenges of scale disparities, and is made accessible through the PulsarX open-source library.

Abstract: The pulsar magnetosphere has only recently been addressed using Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), by deploying a domain-decomposition approach and treating the separatrix and equatorial current sheet as infinitesimally thin discontinuities. However, this baseline requires extensive manual hyperparameter tuning, achieves limited final accuracy and demands several hours of training. We refine this framework by introducing domain-specific neural architectures based on Kolmogorov-Arnold networks, an automated adaptive training pipeline and a physics-based convergence criterion that eliminate the need for manual calibration. The proposed methodology delivers self-consistent axisymmetric magnetosphere solutions with mean squared errors of the PDE residuals at O(1e-6) in double precision - an improvement of two orders of magnitude over the baseline - while achieving convergence in under 20 minutes in single precision. Importantly, the method reliably resolves stellar radii reduced by up to 80% compared to the baseline, overcoming the severe spatial scale disparities that also challenge traditional solvers. Furthermore, by varying the flux that opens to infinity, we provide a correction to the equation that connects it to the equatorial T-point's position. The complete framework is released as the open-source library PulsarX.

</details>


<div id='econ.GN'></div>

# econ.GN [[Back]](#toc)

### [608] [GAGI: A Gini-Adjusted GDP-per-Capita Index for Distribution-Aware Macroeconomic Welfare Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09944)
*Sivasathivel Kandasamy*

Main category: econ.GN

TL;DR: GDP per capita misses key prosperity factors like inequality and inflation. The paper offers the Gini-Adjusted GDP per Capita Index (GAGI), integrating these factors for better macroeconomic monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current macroeconomic measures, like GDP per capita, fail to account for income inequality and inflation impacts, leaving critical gaps in understanding lived economic prosperity.

Method: The authors propose the Gini-Adjusted GDP per Capita Index (GAGI), which adjusts GDP per capita by inequality (using 1-G) and price levels, making it transparent, computationally feasible, and normalized for year-over-year and cross-country analysis.

Result: Using GAGI on G7 economies (2010-2026) reveals that welfare-adjusted prosperity increasingly diverges from GDP growth, particularly post-2022, potentially linked to COVID effects and generative AI adoption.

Conclusion: GAGI is an essential tool to complement GDP monitoring, as measuring aggregate output alone overlooks the distributional impacts and harms of automation and inequality, even during reported economic growth.

Abstract: GDP per capita is the default lens through which governibng bodies track the economic prosperity and consequences of economic events , yet it is blind to two first-order determinants of lived prosperity: income/wealth distribution and inflation impact. Inequality-adjusted income measures are themselves not new but What is missing from the macroeconomic monitoring toolkit specifically is not a welfare concept but an operational monitoring trigger: a statistic minimal enough to compute annually from public data, transparent enough to audit without modelling assumptions, and normalised so that year-on-year, cross-country change ? the quantity a regulator needs to act on? is legible. We assemble such an instrument, the Gini- Adjusted GDP per Capita Index (GAGI): a reproducible, publicly computable formulation that rescales each country's GDP per capita by its inequality-adjustment factor (1-G) and its price level, normalised to a 2010 baseline. GAGI is a general-purpose welfare index, not inherently specific to AI automation, applicable wherever welfare-adjusted prosperity needs tracking. Applying GAGI to the G7 economies over 2010-2026, we show that welfare-adjusted prosperity has diverged persistently and increasingly from headline GDP growth, that the divergence widens sharply after 2022, temporally coincident with, though not, on this evidence alone, demonstrated to be caused by the after effects of COVID and the acceleration of generative-AI deployment. We argue that GAGI is a necessary complement to GDP-based monitoring: any macroeconomic monitoring instrument that tracks only aggregate output will systematically miss the distributional harm that automation can cause even while reported growth remains strong.

</details>


<div id='cs.IR'></div>

# cs.IR [[Back]](#toc)

### [609] [$τ$-Rec: A Verifiable Benchmark for Agentic Recommender Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10156)
*Bharath Sivaram Narasimhan,Karthik R Narasimhan*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: $τ$-Rec is a new benchmark for conversational recommender systems that uses verifiable rewards and a novel testing method to address issues in current evaluation paradigms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the inadequacies of current evaluation methods for conversational recommender systems, which rely on subjective and inconsistent LLM-based judgment.

Method: The paper introduces $τ$-Rec, which employs structured predicates, a reveal-tagged elicitation mechanism, and a pass^k reliability metric to objectively evaluate system performance.

Result: A test of nine configurations across five model families revealed limitations in current systems, with even the best performing model achieving only 57% pass^1 and 38% pass^4 reliability.

Conclusion: $τ$-Rec highlights significant gaps in the deployment readiness of conversational recommender systems and offers a systematic, reproducible benchmark for future research.

Abstract: As recommender systems transition toward agentic, multi-turn conversational interfaces, evaluation paradigms have struggled to keep pace. Current benchmarks often rely on "LLM-as-a-judge" evaluations, which introduce subjectivity, high costs and inconsistency. We present $τ$-Rec, a benchmark for agentic recommender systems that replaces subjective evaluation with verifiable rewards and a reveal-tagged elicitation (RTE) mechanism that controls how task constraints surface during dialogue. By testing agents against structured catalog predicates and employing a pass^k reliability metric, $τ$-Rec provides a systematic test for consistent reasoning. Our evaluation of nine configurations across five model families -- GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Flash, DeepSeek V4 Flash, Qwen3-32B and GPT-5 mini -- reveals a steep reliability cliff, where even the best model achieves only ~57% at pass^1 and ~38% at pass^4, highlighting a critical gap in current conversational agent deployment. All code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/nbharaths/tau-rec.

</details>


### [610] [Selection, Not Salience: The Shape and Limits of Personalization in Social Highlighting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10398)
*Kazuki Nakayashiki,Keisuke Watanabe*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: This paper examines the effectiveness and limitations of personalized reading recommendations and highlights, finding modest personalization benefits primarily at the thematic selection layer of documents, but no gains at sentence-level salience personalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate whether personalization benefits readers and to identify the limits of personalization across different reading contexts, such as documents and sentences.

Method: The study used a social web highlighter and a co-readership identity control to analyze personalization signals at both document and sentence levels. They measured performance through own-versus-other gaps and two-stage personalized auto-highlighting experiments with large language models.

Result: Personalization showed a measurable but modest signal (~+0.13) primarily dominated by stable topic preferences. At the sentence level, personalization failed to outperform impersonal baselines, even with advanced methods. A control-in-negatives bias was also identified and corrected.

Conclusion: Personalization benefits are limited and primarily tied to thematic preferences at the document selection layer, with no significant improvement observed in personalized sentence salience. Aggregating individuals may be more effective than deeper personalization efforts.

Abstract: Does personalizing what a reader sees pay off, and where does it stop? Using a social web highlighter and a co-readership identity control (the same document highlighted by many users, which holds document and topic fixed and asks whether a person's own history predicts their marks better than another reader's does), we map the shape and limits of personalization across reading altitudes. At the document altitude we give the clean, leakage-free, identity-controlled measurement that prior next-document evaluations could only upper-bound: a person's history identifies which documents in a co-reading neighborhood are theirs, with an own-versus-other gap of +0.169 against community negatives and +0.119 against topic-matched hard negatives (both highly significant); a content-based arm suggests the signal is not purely title-driven but is largely thematic. This is comparable to the span-level selection signal (+0.14) from our prior work: the selection signal is of comparable magnitude across altitudes (+0.12 to +0.17), most of it stable topic preference. At the sentence altitude, a two-stage personalized auto-highlight (an impersonal model proposes candidates, a personal model re-ranks them) does not improve on its impersonal baseline: two off-the-shelf zero-shot LLMs, including a frontier model, predict highlight locations worse than a lead baseline, and personal re-ranking is beaten by the salience order even on the highest-recall candidate pool, so the null is not merely a Stage-1 ceiling artifact. Measurable personalization appears primarily at the selection layer: modest (~+0.13), topic-dominated, with no reliable gain at the salience layer. We also surface a control-in-negatives bias that inflated our document gap to a spurious +0.227 until audited. Going beyond the shared salience layer may be better approached by aggregating individuals than by personalizing them harder.

</details>


### [611] [Generative Archetype-Grounded Item Representations for Sequential Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11023)
*Yifan Li,Jiahong Liu,Xinni Zhang,Hao Chen,Yankai Chen,Wenhao Yu,Jianting Chen,Irwin King*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: This paper introduces GenAIR, a framework that integrates generative archetypes and behavioral calibration into sequential recommendation models, showing significant performance improvements over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sequential recommendation struggles with limited item representation quality and a gap between semantic and behavioral data.

Method: GenAIR leverages large language models to create generative archetype embeddings grounded in real-world user behavior through behavioral calibration.

Result: Experiments on three real-world datasets demonstrate GenAIR's superiority over state-of-the-art baseline models and improvements in sequential recommendation performance.
